You still compute p-values, but for the one-sided tests defining whether the CI lies entirely within the equivalence bounds. If the 90% CI lies within [-δ, δ], equivalence is declared at α=0.05 in TOST (because two one-sided tests at 0.05 corresponds to a 90% two-sided CI).
I think I need to restate: A p-value is not P(H0 true). It’s P(test statistic as extreme | H0). Right?
Exactly.
Then what is P(H0 true | data) called?
In Bayesian inference, you’d compute posterior probability of H0 given data, P(H0 | data), using Bayes’ theorem. That requires specifying prior probabilities for H0 and H1 and likelihoods under each.
Can frequentists ever talk about probability of hypotheses?

Traditional frequentist inference treats hypotheses/parameters as fixed, not random, so it doesn’t assign probabilities to them. Some frequentist-adjacent approaches (like confidence distributions) try to give probability-like statements, but they’re not the same as Bayesian posteriors.
Are p-values “wrong” then?
Not inherently. They are valid tools when used correctly and interpreted properly. Problems arise when they are over-interpreted, used with undisclosed flexibility, or treated as definitive proof.
I’ve also seen “p = 0.000” in outputs. That can’t be right, can it?

It’s rounding. The p-value is never exactly 0 under continuous distributions; it can be extremely small, like < 1e-16. Software sometimes prints 0.000 when it rounds to three decimals. Better is to report p < 0.001 or p = 2.3×10^-8, etc.
Another phrase that confuses me: “reject the null.” Does that mean we accept the alternative?

In the Neyman–Pearson framework, you “reject H0” or “fail to reject H0.” You typically do not “accept H0,” because failing to reject could be due to low power. Similarly, rejecting H0 doesn’t automatically mean a specific alternative is true; it just means the data are inconsistent with H0 at the chosen α.
So “fail to reject” is not the same as “the null is true.”
Correct. It could mean the data are not informative enough, or the effect is smaller than what you can detect.
What about power? How does power relate to p-values?

Power is P(reject H0 | H1 is true). It depends on the true effect size, sample size, variance, and α. If power is low, then even if there’s an effect, p-values will often be large. That’s why non-significant results are ambiguous without power considerations.
Can we connect power to the distribution of p-values under H1?
Yes. Under H1, p-values tend to be small, but the exact distribution depends on the effect size and test. Power at α is simply P(p ≤ α | H1). So power is the CDF of p at α under the alternative.
That’s neat. So power is basically the fraction of p-values below α when H1 holds.
Exactly.
Is there a way to estimate the chance that a “significant” result will replicate?

There are approaches, but it’s not straightforward. Replication probability depends on the true effect size, study design, and selection effects. A rough idea: if you estimate an effect and its standard error, you can model what a replication’s test statistic might look like. But using p-values alone to predict replication is unreliable.
People sometimes say “p-values are not reproducible.” What does that mean?

P-values are random variables; they vary a lot from sample to sample, even when the true effect is the same. Especially near the threshold, p-values can bounce around widely, so a p=0.04 in one sample might become p=0.20 in another, even if the underlying effect exists.
Can you illustrate that with a simple scenario?

Suppose true standardized effect is modest, and your sample size yields power around 50% at α=0.05. That means even when the effect is real, half the time you won’t get p<0.05. Among the times you do get p<0.05, the observed effect sizes are often inflated (winner’s curse), and p-values can still vary from tiny to barely below 0.05.
Winner’s curse?

It’s selection bias: if you only look at results that pass a threshold, the ones that pass tend to have overestimated effects due to random noise pushing them upward. This is especially pronounced with low power.
So if I see a significant p, I should suspect the effect size might be exaggerated?
Often, yes, particularly if the study is underpowered and publication/selection is based on significance.
If p-values are so tricky, why not just use effect sizes and confidence intervals?

Effect sizes and CIs are generally more informative, but they still depend on assumptions and can be misinterpreted. Also, decision-making sometimes demands a threshold. But shifting emphasis toward estimation and uncertainty typically improves interpretation.
How do I interpret a confidence interval correctly, then?

As a range produced by a procedure that, under repeated sampling from the same process, covers the true parameter 95% of the time. Practically: if the model assumptions are reasonable, values outside the interval are less compatible with the observed data than those inside, at the 5% level (in a certain sense).
“Compatible” sounds vague.

It’s intentionally softer than “probable.” It means: under the assumed model, parameter values inside the CI would not be rejected by a two-sided test at α=0.05, whereas values outside would be rejected.
So a CI is like the set of parameter values that would yield p>0.05 if tested?
For many standard cases, yes: the 95% CI corresponds to all parameter values not rejected at the 5% level by a two-sided test. That’s called a confidence set inversion.
Another thing: if I get p=0.04, does that mean the result is “unlikely due to random chance”?

Under H0, yes: such a result would occur about 4% of the time. But randomness exists under any hypothesis; “due to chance” isn’t a distinct cause. The question is whether the null model adequately explains the data.
Is there a common way to express that without misleading?
You can say: “If the null were true, we would observe data this extreme or more about 4% of the time.” That’s faithful.
What about “the probability of the observed effect under H0”?

That’s not quite right, because the p-value is not the probability of the observed effect, but of observing an effect at least as extreme (according to the test statistic). Also, effect estimates have continuous distributions, so the probability of exactly that estimate is not meaningful.
I’m still uneasy. If p is defined under H0, then it’s like we’re assuming what we’re trying to disprove. Isn’t that circular?

It can feel that way, but it’s a standard logic of hypothesis testing: assume H0 to derive the distribution of a statistic, then see whether the observed statistic is typical or atypical under that assumption. If it’s very atypical, you take that as evidence that the assumption may be wrong (or that something else is off).
Kind of like proof by contradiction?
Similar in spirit: you assume H0 and see if it leads to an implausible observation.
How do assumptions come into this? Like normality assumptions in a t-test.

The p-value is valid only if the test statistic’s distribution under H0 is correctly specified. For a t-test, assumptions include independent observations, approximate normality of errors (or large n so CLT helps), and correct variance modeling (equal variances if you use the pooled t-test; or use Welch’s t-test otherwise). If assumptions fail, the p-value may not have the advertised properties.
So a p-value can be “wrong” if the model is wrong?

Yes. It can be miscalibrated: under H0, it may not be uniform, and the Type I error rate may not be α. Robust methods, permutation tests, bootstrap, or nonparametric tests can help depending on the situation.
Permutation tests give p-values too, right?

Yes. A permutation p-value is computed by comparing the observed test statistic to the distribution of that statistic under all (or many randomly sampled) permutations consistent with H0. It often relies on fewer parametric assumptions, but it still relies on an exchangeability assumption (the permutations represent the null distribution).
Are permutation p-values more “honest”?

They can be more robust in some settings, especially when the parametric distribution is questionable. But they still share the fundamental interpretation: probability of extreme statistic under H0, given the permutation scheme.
What about bootstrap p-values?

Bootstrap can be used to approximate sampling distributions, but p-values from bootstrap require careful construction; naive bootstrap can be biased. Often bootstrap is used more for confidence intervals than for p-values, though there are bootstrap tests.
If I want the probability that the hypothesis is true, should I always use Bayesian methods?

Bayesian methods are the standard way to compute probabilities about hypotheses or parameters given data. But they require specifying priors, which can be contentious. Also, Bayesian outputs can still be misused. It’s not automatically “better,” but it answers different questions.
You said earlier p-values are uniform under H0. Does that mean p=0.5 is as likely as p=0.01 under H0?
Exactly. Under H0, any p in [0,1] is equally likely (in the continuous case). So seeing p=0.01 is not impossible under H0; it happens 1% of the time.
So if I run 100 true-null tests, I expect about one p-value around 0.01?
You expect about one p-value below 0.01. The minimum p-value among 100 is often quite small even if all nulls are true.
That’s another thing: if I test many things, I will get small p-values just by chance.
Yes, and that’s why multiple testing correction or careful pre-specification is important.
Let’s talk about “statistical significance” again. What does it formally mean?

Formally, given a test with significance level α, a result is “statistically significant at level α” if the p-value is ≤ α (equivalently, the test statistic falls in the rejection region). This guarantees that the probability of incorrectly rejecting H0 is at most α, under repeated sampling and assuming H0 is true and assumptions hold.
Is α always 0.05?
No. 0.05 is common, but you can choose 0.01, 0.10, or other levels depending on costs of errors, conventions, or multiple testing. Some contexts use much smaller thresholds.
Are there situations where 0.10 makes sense?
It can, particularly in exploratory analyses or when missing a real effect is considered more costly than a false alarm. But you should be transparent about the choice and interpret accordingly.
So p-values don’t encode the “cost” of errors; α does.
Correct. The p-value is a measure computed from data; α is a decision threshold reflecting tolerable Type I error.
What about Type II error?
Type II error rate is β = P(fail to reject H0 | H1 true). Power is 1 - β. You can control β by choosing sample size, effect size of interest, and α.
Is there an equivalent “p-value for Type II error”?

Not really. There are concepts like “p-value for equivalence testing” or “observed power” (which is generally not recommended), but Type II error is about the test’s behavior under alternatives, not a single observed dataset.
Observed power is bad?

“Observed power” computed from the observed effect size tends to be a one-to-one transformation of the p-value and doesn’t provide new information. It’s also misleading because it conditions on a noisy estimate.
So better to do prospective power calculations?
Yes: plan sample size based on a smallest effect size of interest and desired power, before collecting data.
But if I already have the data, power isn’t helpful?

Post-hoc, it’s better to report the estimate and CI. If you want to discuss sensitivity, you can do “detectable effect size” analyses or compute power curves for various hypothetical true effects, but avoid interpreting “observed power” as if it’s a property of the study independent of the result.
Another thing I see: “p < 0.05, therefore we reject the null and accept the alternative.” But there could be many alternatives.

Exactly. Rejecting H0 just says “H0 is inconsistent with the data under the test.” It doesn’t specify what the true parameter is, nor does it validate a particular alternative model. That’s why estimation and model checking matter.
Are p-values affected by measurement error?

Yes, measurement error typically increases variance and can reduce power, leading to larger p-values (harder to detect effects). But depending on structure, it can also bias estimates, affecting p-values in complicated ways.
So p is not just “signal vs noise,” it’s also “quality of measurement and model.”
Right.
Let’s talk about “null hypothesis significance testing” as a whole. Is it Fisher or Neyman–Pearson?

Historically: - Fisher emphasized p-values as a measure of evidence against H0, without strict accept/reject rules. - Neyman–Pearson emphasized decision rules with fixed α and β, focusing on long-run error control. Modern NHST often mixes the two: people compute p-values (Fisher) but then apply rigid thresholds (Neyman–Pearson) and interpret them as evidence/probability (neither). That mix contributes to confusion.
So when someone says “p = 0.03, we reject H0,” that’s more Neyman–Pearson?
The reject/fail-to-reject language is Neyman–Pearson style, but using the exact p-value as a graded measure is more Fisher. In practice they’re blended.
If I want to be precise, what should I say when reporting a p-value?

A precise statement: “Under the null hypothesis and model assumptions, the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme or more extreme than the one observed is p = ...” And then pair it with effect size and CI, and avoid implying that p is the probability H0 is true.
But that’s a mouthful.
It is. A shorter version that’s still okay: “The data are inconsistent with H0 at the 5% level (p = 0.03).” The key is not to translate it into probability the hypothesis is true.
What about “highly significant” for p < 0.001?
“Highly significant” is common but can be misleading because it sounds like “highly important.” If you use it, clarify you mean “very small p-value,” not large effect.
I want to understand “as extreme or more extreme.” In the coin example, why did we include 16,17,18,19,20 heads?

Because the test statistic is “number of heads” and the alternative is “different from 0.5.” Observing 16 heads is even more incompatible with fairness than 15 heads, so it belongs in the tail region. The p-value sums probabilities of all outcomes at least as incompatible as the observed one.
But why also include the other side, like 5 or fewer heads?
In a two-sided test, extremeness is in both directions: too many heads or too few heads both contradict fairness. So you include both tails.
How do we define “equally extreme” on both sides for discrete distributions?

That’s a subtle point. For discrete tests, there are multiple definitions of a two-sided p-value: - Doubling the one-sided tail probability (simple but can exceed 1 or be conservative). - Summing probabilities of outcomes with probability ≤ P(observed) under H0 (the “exact” two-sided p-value used in Fisher’s exact test, etc.). - Using mid-p adjustments. Different definitions can yield slightly different p-values.
So even p-values aren’t uniquely defined sometimes.
Correct, especially in discrete settings.
Another thing: if p-values are uniform under H0, then the expected p-value under H0 is 0.5?
Yes. For Uniform(0,1), the mean is 0.5 and median is 0.5.
Under H1, the expected p-value is less than 0.5?
Generally yes, if the test has some power. The distribution shifts toward 0.
Can p-values be used to estimate how many hypotheses are true in a large set?
There are methods to estimate π0, the proportion of true null hypotheses, from the p-value distribution (e.g., Storey’s method). That’s used in FDR contexts.
Interesting. So p-values have legitimate uses besides “is it significant.”
Yes, in multiple testing, calibration checks, model diagnostics, and more.
Speaking of model diagnostics: are p-values used in goodness-of-fit tests?

Yes. In goodness-of-fit tests (like chi-square tests, Kolmogorov–Smirnov, etc.), the p-value assesses whether the observed discrepancy between data and model is unusually large under the model. But these p-values also depend on sample size: with large n, tiny deviations from the model can be significant.
So a small p-value in a goodness-of-fit test might just mean the model is slightly wrong, not catastrophically wrong.
Exactly. With large samples, almost any model is technically false, so p-values often become very small. That’s why practical diagnostics (residual plots, effect sizes of misfit) matter.
Is that related to the idea “all models are wrong”?
Yes. Statistical models are approximations. Hypothesis tests can detect tiny departures from idealized assumptions, especially with large data.
Then what’s the point of testing?
Testing can still be useful to detect meaningful departures or to compare models, but it should be paired with judgment about magnitude and relevance.
Another confusion: If p is computed under H0, why do we use it to decide about H1?

Because in frequentist testing, you design a rule that controls false positives under H0. If the data fall in a region that rarely occurs under H0, you reject H0. It’s a way of controlling the risk of incorrect rejection while being sensitive to alternatives (power).
But it doesn’t tell me the probability H1 is true.
Correct.
Suppose I have p = 0.2. Can I say “there’s no effect”?

Not necessarily. p = 0.2 means the data are not very incompatible with H0; you don’t have strong evidence against H0. But you might also not have enough precision to detect an effect. Look at the confidence interval: if it includes both practically important effects and near-zero effects, the result is inconclusive. If the CI is narrow and centered near zero, that supports “no meaningful effect.”
So I should interpret non-significant results with CIs, not just p.
Yes.
What about “p = 0.2 implies the null is likely”?

That’s also not justified. Under H0, p-values are uniform, so p=0.2 is not particularly supportive of H0 compared to other p-values; it’s just not small. Evidence for H0 is better assessed with equivalence tests, Bayes factors, or likelihood ratios.
Wait, p=0.9 isn’t evidence for H0 either?

Not in the usual sense. A very large p-value can occur under H0, but also under H1 if the test is low power or if the true effect is in a direction the test isn’t sensitive to. Some people interpret very large p-values as “data are very compatible with H0,” but it’s still not a probability that H0 is true.
So p-values are asymmetric: small p is evidence against H0, but large p isn’t strong evidence for H0.
That’s a good way to think about it. P-values are designed to detect inconsistency with H0, not to confirm H0.
Is there any frequentist method to get evidence for H0?

Equivalence/non-inferiority tests can provide evidence that effects are smaller than a meaningful threshold. Also, confidence intervals that lie entirely within a negligible region support practical equivalence. For “exactly zero,” frequentist methods don’t usually provide evidence in that sense.
Let’s dig into the math a bit. How exactly do you compute a p-value from a test statistic?

General template: 1) Choose a test statistic T = T(data) such that larger values indicate more disagreement with H0 (or use |T| for two-sided). 2) Determine the distribution of T under H0: the CDF F0(t) = P(T ≤ t | H0). 3) Compute observed t_obs = T(data). 4) Compute p-value as tail probability: - Right-tailed: p = P(T ≥ t_obs | H0) = 1 - F0(t_obs). - Left-tailed: p = P(T ≤ t_obs | H0) = F0(t_obs). - Two-tailed (symmetric): p = P(|T| ≥ |t_obs| | H0). In practice you may use exact distribution, asymptotic approximation, or simulation.
How does this relate to t-tests?
In a one-sample t-test, T = (x̄ - μ0) / (s/√n). Under H0 and normality, T follows a t distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom. Then p is computed from that t distribution.
And for large n, t distribution approximates normal?
Yes, as df → ∞, t approaches standard normal.
You mentioned assumptions. If the data aren’t normal, is the t-test invalid?

Not necessarily. The t-test is fairly robust to moderate non-normality when n is not too small, especially for symmetric distributions, due to the central limit theorem. But heavy tails, strong skewness with small n, or outliers can distort Type I error and power. Alternatives include nonparametric tests (Wilcoxon), robust t-tests, or transformations.
If I use a nonparametric test, the p-value still has the same interpretation?

Yes: it’s still the probability of getting a test statistic as extreme under the null (as defined for that test). The null might be phrased differently (e.g., equality of distributions or median), but the p-value interpretation remains conditional on the null.
Another topic: What is “statistical significance” vs “confidence level”? Are they complements?

They are linked but not complements. Significance level α corresponds to confidence level 1 - α for a two-sided CI in many settings. If you test at α=0.05 two-sided, that corresponds to a 95% CI. But “significance” is about rejection; “confidence level” is about interval coverage.
I’ve seen p-values reported with stars: * p<0.05, ** p<0.01, *** p<0.001. Is that meaningful?
It’s a shorthand, but it encourages threshold thinking and can obscure effect sizes. It’s okay as a quick indicator but shouldn’t be the main focus.
Are there any good rules of thumb for interpreting p-values?

If you must use heuristics: - Very small p (e.g., <0.001) suggests strong incompatibility with H0 (assuming no multiple testing/p-hacking and assumptions hold). - p near 0.05 is weak-to-moderate evidence against H0 and is sensitive to design choices. - Large p suggests insufficient evidence against H0, not evidence for H0. But always pair with effect size and CI, and consider context like multiple testing and power.
But I’m not supposed to mention any context. I just want a general understanding.

General understanding: p-values quantify extremeness under H0; thresholds control long-run false positives; p-values do not give probabilities of hypotheses; and they’re sensitive to sample size, assumptions, and analytic flexibility.
What about “p-value is the probability that the result is due to chance.” You said that’s vague. Can you explain why it’s wrong in a more formal way?

Because “due to chance” is not a well-defined event. In a probabilistic model, chance is always present. The p-value is not P(H0 true), not P(result caused by random noise), and not P(rejecting was a mistake). It’s P(T ≥ t_obs | H0) (or two-sided variant). That’s a conditional probability about hypothetical repetitions when H0 holds.
Another phrasing: “p-value is the probability of observing the data given that the null is true.” Is that closer?

Still not quite. That would be P(data | H0), which is a likelihood (or probability/density) of the observed data. The p-value is P(data at least as extreme as observed | H0), which is a tail area, not the probability of the observed data.
Why does that distinction matter?

Because tail areas depend on outcomes you did not observe and on the definition of “extreme.” Two different test statistics can lead to different tail areas even with the same observed data. Likelihood focuses on what you observed; p-values incorporate a reference set of unobserved outcomes.
Is that why likelihoodists criticize p-values?

Yes, one criticism is that p-values violate the likelihood principle: they depend on the sampling plan and include probabilities of unobserved data. Likelihood-based inference and Bayesian inference adhere more closely to the likelihood principle.
What’s the likelihood principle?

It says that all evidence about model parameters contained in the data is in the likelihood function, and that two datasets with proportional likelihood functions should lead to the same inference, regardless of sampling plans. Frequentist p-values can differ under different sampling plans even if likelihood is the same.
That sounds important. Can you give an example?

A classic example compares: - Fixed sample size binomial sampling (flip coin n times). - Negative binomial sampling (flip until you get a fixed number of heads). You can end up with the same likelihood for p given the observed counts, but p-values can differ because the set of possible outcomes under the sampling plan differs.
So p-values aren’t just about the observed data; they’re about what could have happened.
Exactly.
Is that bad?

It depends on your philosophy. If you care about long-run error rates under a procedure, you must consider what could have happened under that procedure. If you care about evidence in the observed data alone, you might prefer likelihood or Bayesian measures.
But many people want a single number that tells them “is it real.”
That’s understandable, but statistics rarely gives that without assumptions and tradeoffs. Even Bayesian posterior probabilities depend on priors.
If I wanted a Bayesian analog of p-value, what is it?

There are Bayesian p-values (posterior predictive p-values), but they have different properties and interpretations. Alternatively, Bayes factors compare hypotheses directly, and posterior probabilities quantify belief given priors.
Posterior predictive p-values—are they the same as frequentist?

No. Posterior predictive p-values compute the probability that replicated data (generated from the posterior predictive distribution) would be more extreme than observed, according to some discrepancy measure. They tend to be conservative and are used for model checking rather than hypothesis testing.
So it’s more like “does the model fit” than “is the parameter zero.”
Often, yes.

I’m trying to summarize: p-value is a conditional probability under H0. It measures how extreme the data are compared to what H0 predicts. Small p suggests incompatibility with H0, but does not tell probability H0 is true or effect is important.
Perfect summary.
Yet I still feel like: if p is 0.01, then under H0 I would see this only 1% of the time. So isn’t it rational to doubt H0?

It is rational to treat it as evidence against H0, yes. The key is to avoid over-interpreting it as a probability of truth. Evidence and probability are related but not identical without a full probabilistic model over hypotheses.
If I had to explain to someone in one sentence, what would I say?
“A p-value is the chance, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of getting results at least as extreme as what you observed.”
And what is the correct statement for “there’s a 95% chance the alternative is true”?

You can’t say that from p alone. To say something like that you’d need Bayesian posterior probability: “Given the data and prior assumptions, the posterior probability of H1 is 95%.” But that’s a different analysis.
Can p-values ever be converted to that kind of probability?
Not uniquely. Without specifying priors and an alternative model, many different posterior probabilities can correspond to the same p-value.
So p is not a universal measure.
Right. It’s one tool in a larger toolkit.
What about “significance” vs “confidence” vs “credibility.” People mix these words.

Briefly: - Significance: relates to hypothesis tests and α thresholds. - Confidence: frequentist interval coverage. - Credible: Bayesian probability statements about parameters given data and prior. Mixing them causes confusion.
I’m also confused about “null hypothesis.” Is it always “no effect”?
Not always. H0 can be any precise statement: a parameter equals some value, a distribution has some form, two variables are independent, etc. “No effect” is common but not required.
And “alternative hypothesis” is what you want to show?

It’s the set of parameter values you consider as the alternative to H0. It might be two-sided (not equal) or one-sided (greater/less). In Neyman–Pearson, the alternative is essential for defining the most powerful tests; in Fisher’s approach, H1 is often left unspecified.
Does the p-value depend on the alternative?
It depends on whether the test is one-sided or two-sided and on the choice of statistic, which is often motivated by the alternative. But given a chosen test, the p-value is computed under H0.
So p-value isn’t “probability of data under H1.”
Correct.
Is there any scenario where p-values can be interpreted as posterior probabilities?
Only under very specific assumptions and calibrations, and even then it’s delicate. Generally, no.

Let’s do another numerical example. Suppose I have an estimated mean difference of 2, standard error 1. So t = 2. p about 0.05 two-sided. If standard error was 0.5, t=4, p tiny. But the effect estimate is still 2. So p changes because precision changes.
Exactly. That’s why p-values are not measures of effect magnitude; they mix magnitude and precision.
So should I prefer standardized effect sizes?

Standardized effect sizes (like Cohen’s d) can help compare across scales, but they also have limitations and depend on variance. The best practice is to report the raw effect (in meaningful units) plus uncertainty, and optionally standardized measures.
If I’m comparing two groups, what’s the best summary?
Typically: difference in means (or medians), its CI, perhaps a standardized effect, and a p-value if you’re doing testing. Also consider assumptions and whether the design supports the model.
I keep wanting a “probability it’s true.” But that’s not what frequentist tools give.
Right.
Okay, last angle: If p-value is the probability of data as extreme given H0, then smaller p means less compatible with H0. Is “compatibility” a formal concept?

It can be formalized via p-values as a measure of how extreme the statistic is under H0; some statisticians describe this as “compatibility” between data and model. But it’s not a probability of the model; it’s a tail-area measure.
Could I say “p=0.04 means the data are relatively incompatible with H0”?
Yes, that’s fair phrasing.
And “p=0.5 means data are quite compatible with H0”?
“Not incompatible” is safer. High p doesn’t necessarily mean “supports H0,” but it indicates no evidence against H0 from that test.
Got it. I think my main misconception was flipping the conditional probability.
That’s the central one.
If you had to write the incorrect and correct statements side by side, what would that look like?
Incorrect: “p = 0.03 means there is a 3% chance H0 is true.” Correct: “p = 0.03 means that, if H0 were true, the chance of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as observed is 3%.”
Thanks. I’m going to try to explain it to myself again: It’s not probability the hypothesis is true, it’s probability of seeing data this weird if the hypothesis were true.
Exactly.
One more question: If p-values are uniform under H0, then does that mean a p-value of 0.99 is just as likely as 0.01 under H0?

Yes. Under H0, both occur with probability 1% in the sense that P(p ≤ 0.01)=1% and P(p ≥ 0.99)=1%. Extreme high p-values are as rare as extreme low ones under H0, though they’re rarely used as evidence for anything.
That’s interesting. So an extremely high p-value is also “surprising” under H0?

It’s rare, but it’s “surprising” in a different way. It means the test statistic fell extremely close to the null expectation. That can happen by chance, or it might suggest issues like overfitting, data dependence, or overly conservative variance estimates. But typically we don’t treat it as strong evidence for H0.
So both ends are rare, but only the low end is used for rejection.
Right, because the rejection region is set in the tail(s) corresponding to disagreement with H0, not agreement.
Okay. I think I’m finally less confused.
Can you explain the Riemann Hypothesis to someone who knows calculus but not complex analysis?

The Riemann Hypothesis (RH) is a statement about where the “nontrivial zeros” of a certain function called the Riemann zeta function live, and why that location governs how regularly prime numbers are distributed. Here’s a high-level path that stays as close as possible to familiar calculus ideas: 1) Prime numbers and counting them Let π(x) be the number of primes ≤ x. For example, π(10) = 4 (2,3,5,7). A huge theme in number theory is: how big is π(x) approximately, and how big is the error in that approximation? 2) The Prime Number Theorem (PNT) One key approximation is that π(x) is close to x / log x (natural log). More precisely, π(x) ~ x / log x as x → ∞. A better approximation is the logarithmic integral li(x) = ∫₂ˣ dt / log t. The PNT says π(x) ~ li(x) too. 3) A function that encodes primes: ζ(s) The zeta function is initially defined for real s > 1 by an infinite series: ζ(s) = 1 + 1/2^s + 1/3^s + 1/4^s + ... This is like a “weighted count” of integers where bigger integers contribute less. 4) Euler product links ζ(s) to primes For s > 1, ζ(s) factors into an infinite product over primes p: ζ(s) = ∏_{p prime} 1 / (1 - p^{-s}) This identity is basically equivalent to unique factorization. If you expand the product as a geometric series in each factor, you get all integers exactly once. 5) Zeros of ζ(s) control prime distribution There is a deep connection (made precise by “explicit formulas”) that says the error term in approximations to prime-counting functions is influenced by the zeros of ζ(s), once ζ(s) is extended to complex numbers. 6) What RH says After extending ζ(s) to complex inputs s = σ + it, it has “trivial zeros” at negative even integers (-2, -4, -6, …). The other zeros—nontrivial zeros—lie in a vertical strip 0 < σ < 1, called the critical strip. Riemann Hypothesis: Every nontrivial zero has real part σ = 1/2. So they all lie on the “critical line” Re(s) = 1/2. 7) Why that matters If RH is true, primes are distributed as regularly as allowed by known constraints: the error term in prime counting is near-optimal. If RH is false, there would be zeros off the critical line, and that would force larger irregularities in prime distribution. If you want, I can explain: - what “analytic continuation” means in an intuitive way, - what complex numbers add here, - what an “explicit formula” looks like (in a simplified form), - or how RH gives concrete bounds like π(x) = li(x) + O(x^{1/2} log x).
I’ve heard about analytic continuation but don’t really get it. Can you explain it without complex analysis machinery?

Think of analytic continuation as “extending a formula beyond where its original definition obviously makes sense,” in a way that’s forced by consistency. A calculus-friendly analogy: 1) Power series as defining functions beyond an initial expression Take the geometric series: for |x| < 1, 1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + ... = 1/(1 - x). The left side only converges for |x| < 1, but the right side makes sense for all x ≠ 1. In a way, 1/(1 - x) is an extension of the sum beyond its convergence radius, and it’s the only “reasonable” extension that matches inside |x| < 1. 2) Another example: the factorial and Γ(s) Factorial n! is defined for positive integers. The Gamma function Γ(s) extends it to non-integers, satisfying Γ(n+1)=n!. That’s like continuation from discrete points to a smooth function. 3) For ζ(s) The series ζ(s) = Σ_{n≥1} n^{-s} converges when Re(s) > 1. If you only use that series, ζ(s) is undefined for Re(s) ≤ 1. But there are other expressions—integrals, functional equations, transformed series—that agree with the original series when Re(s) > 1 and also produce finite values elsewhere (except at s=1 where there is a real singularity). So analytic continuation is: find a new expression that matches the old one on the overlap region and defines the function on a larger region. Why it’s “forced”: If you demand the function be analytic (complex differentiable) and match on any region with accumulation points, then the extension is unique. You don’t need to prove that uniqueness right now; intuitively, analytic functions are extremely rigid—knowing them on a tiny region pins them down everywhere you can reach without crossing singularities.
Okay, but if we’re avoiding complex analysis, how do we even talk about zeros off the real line? Why do complex zeros matter for primes, which are real integers?

Even though primes are integers, the tools that encode them naturally lead to functions of a complex variable because complex exponentials and oscillations are the right language for “frequency analysis” of arithmetic patterns. A bridge from calculus/real analysis: 1) Fourier analysis analogy In Fourier analysis, you represent a real function f(x) using sinusoids e^{itx} (or sin/cos). Those are complex-valued, but they let you analyze oscillations and error terms. The frequencies t determine how f behaves. 2) Mellin transform analogy The zeta function is closely related to a transform like a Mellin transform, which uses x^{s-1} as kernel. When s is complex, x^{σ+it} = x^σ · x^{it} = x^σ · e^{it log x}. That e^{it log x} is oscillatory in log x, like a Fourier mode in log-scale. So complex s encode oscillations in prime counting across scales. 3) Why zeros matter In many transform settings, poles/zeros of a transform correspond to growth rates and oscillatory components of the original counting function. Roughly, if ζ(s) has a zero at s = ρ, then terms like x^{ρ}/ρ show up in explicit formulas for prime-counting functions. If ρ = σ + it, then x^{ρ} = x^σ e^{it log x}, an oscillation of amplitude x^σ. Thus the real part σ controls the size (amplitude growth/decay), while the imaginary part t controls the oscillation frequency. RH says σ = 1/2 for nontrivial zeros, which means the oscillations are about size x^{1/2} (up to logs), which is “small” compared to the main term x.
Can you show me a simplified “explicit formula” that makes that connection more concrete?

A commonly presented version uses the Chebyshev function ψ(x), which is a weighted prime-counting function: ψ(x) = Σ_{p^k ≤ x} log p (sum over prime powers p^k up to x). This is often smoother than π(x). A simplified explicit formula (ignoring some technicalities about how to interpret at discontinuities) looks like: ψ(x) = x - Σ_{ρ} (x^{ρ}/ρ) - log(2π) - (1/2) log(1 - x^{-2}) where the sum is over nontrivial zeros ρ of ζ(s). Interpretation: - The main term is x. - Each zero ρ contributes an oscillatory correction term x^{ρ}/ρ. - The remaining terms are relatively small or explicit. Now, if ρ = σ + it, then x^{ρ} = x^σ e^{it log x}. The magnitude is about x^σ / |ρ|. So the largest σ among zeros controls the worst-case size of fluctuations in ψ(x) around x. RH says σ = 1/2 for all those zeros, so the sum of correction terms should be about size x^{1/2} times some logs, not bigger.
So if RH were false and there was a zero with real part 0.9, then ψ(x) would have fluctuations like x^{0.9}? That seems huge.

Exactly: a zero with real part σ close to 1 would create a term of size roughly x^{σ}/|ρ|, which would dwarf the “expected” square-root scale fluctuations and cause much larger irregularities. There’s a subtlety: contributions from many zeros can cancel somewhat, but a single zero with σ > 1/2 would generally force error terms at least on the order of x^{σ} infinitely often. In fact, there are rigorous statements: if there is a zero with real part β, then certain error terms in prime counting cannot be smaller than about x^{β} in a suitable sense. So RH is a statement that the “largest real part” among nontrivial zeros is 1/2, giving the best possible global regularity.
You mentioned the “critical strip” 0 < σ < 1. Why do zeros have to lie there?

There are two key facts: 1) No zeros for Re(s) > 1 In the region Re(s) > 1, ζ(s) = ∏_{p} (1 - p^{-s})^{-1}. Each factor (1 - p^{-s})^{-1} is finite and nonzero since |p^{-s}| = p^{-σ} < 1, so 1 - p^{-s} ≠ 0. The product converges and cannot be zero. So ζ(s) ≠ 0 for σ > 1. 2) No zeros for Re(s) < 0 except the trivial ones After analytic continuation and using the functional equation (which relates ζ(s) to ζ(1 - s) with some Gamma and sine factors), one can show zeros occur at negative even integers (trivial zeros), and apart from those, the nontrivial zeros are in 0 < σ < 1. Without going deep into the functional equation, the rough idea is: the continuation expresses ζ(s) as something like a product of factors, and some factors introduce zeros at -2, -4, ...; the rest of the zeros are “reflections” of zeros inside the strip. So the interesting zeros are confined to the critical strip because of these analytic properties.
Can you state the functional equation in a way I can parse?

One standard form is: ζ(s) = 2^s π^{s-1} sin(πs/2) Γ(1 - s) ζ(1 - s). This equation relates ζ(s) to ζ(1 - s). It’s powerful because: - It extends information from the region where the series converges (σ > 1) to the region σ < 0. - The factor sin(πs/2) has zeros at s = 0, ±2, ±4, ...; combined with Γ(1 - s), it produces the trivial zeros at negative even integers. There is also a “completed zeta function”: ξ(s) = (1/2) s(s-1) π^{-s/2} Γ(s/2) ζ(s), which satisfies a cleaner symmetry: ξ(s) = ξ(1 - s). This symmetry implies the zeros are symmetric with respect to the critical line σ = 1/2.
What does it mean for zeros to be symmetric with respect to σ = 1/2?

If ρ is a zero of ξ(s) (equivalently a nontrivial zero of ζ(s)), then 1 - ρ is also a zero, because ξ(s) = ξ(1 - s). So if ρ = σ + it is a zero, then 1 - ρ = (1 - σ) - it is also a zero. Also, because coefficients in the relevant expansions are real, complex zeros come in conjugate pairs: if ρ is a zero then \bar{ρ} = σ - it is also a zero. Combine these: - ρ = σ + it - \bar{ρ} = σ - it - 1 - ρ = 1 - σ - it - 1 - \bar{ρ} = 1 - σ + it So zeros are mirrored across σ = 1/2 (since σ and 1 - σ are paired). RH says all zeros already have σ = 1/2, so the mirror is the same point.
Can you connect RH to a concrete bound on π(x) or ψ(x)?

A classical consequence is a strong error bound for ψ(x): Assuming RH: ψ(x) = x + O(x^{1/2} log^2 x). There are refinements, but this is a standard headline bound. For π(x), one gets: π(x) = li(x) + O(x^{1/2} log x). The key point is the exponent 1/2: the error is roughly square-root in x (times logs). That is dramatically smaller than the main term ~ x/log x. Without RH, the best unconditional error bounds are weaker (larger error terms), though still much smaller than x.
Where do those log factors come from? Why not just O(x^{1/2})?

Two main sources: 1) Summing over many zeros Even if each zero contributes about x^{1/2}/|ρ|, you have infinitely many zeros. Their contributions don’t add in absolute value nicely; you need estimates on how many zeros there are up to a given height (imaginary part), and you sum something like Σ x^{1/2}/|ρ|. That produces logs. 2) Technical smoothing and truncation The explicit formula is typically handled by truncating the sum over zeros up to some height T and bounding the remainder. Optimizing T often introduces logarithmic terms. So the logs reflect both the density of zeros and the cost of controlling the infinite sum.
How many zeros are there up to height T?

Let N(T) be the number of nontrivial zeros ρ = β + iγ with 0 < γ ≤ T (counted with multiplicity). There is an asymptotic formula: N(T) = (T / 2π) log(T / 2π) - (T / 2π) + O(log T). So the number of zeros grows roughly like (T/2π) log T. This density is part of why sums over zeros bring in log factors.
Is RH equivalent to some statement that looks more “real-variable,” like an inequality?

Yes—there are many equivalent statements. A few well-known flavors: 1) Error term equivalences RH is equivalent to the statement that: ψ(x) = x + O(x^{1/2} log^2 x). And similarly for related functions like θ(x) = Σ_{p≤x} log p. 2) Bounds on the Mertens function Define the Möbius function μ(n): μ(n)=0 if n has a squared prime factor; otherwise μ(n)=(-1)^k where k is the number of prime factors. The Mertens function is: M(x) = Σ_{n≤x} μ(n). RH is equivalent to: M(x) = O(x^{1/2+ε}) for every ε > 0. (There are subtle distinctions about implied constants, but that’s the idea.) 3) Growth of partial sums of the Liouville function and other multiplicative functions Various bounds on summatory functions of arithmetic functions are equivalent or closely related. These are “real” statements about sums up to x, no complex numbers visible, but they encode the same spectral information as the zeros.
The Möbius function always felt mysterious. Why does it show up here?

Because μ is the inverse of the constant 1 function under Dirichlet convolution, and it’s tied to 1/ζ(s). Specifically, for Re(s) > 1: 1/ζ(s) = Σ_{n=1}^\infty μ(n) / n^s. This is analogous to how 1/(1-x) = Σ x^n. Here ζ(s) is like a generating function for 1, and its reciprocal generates μ. So zeros of ζ(s) correspond to poles of 1/ζ(s), which correspond to growth/oscillation in partial sums of μ(n). That’s why RH can be reframed as a bound on M(x).
Can you briefly remind me what Dirichlet convolution is?

For arithmetic functions f and g (functions on positive integers), their Dirichlet convolution (f * g)(n) is: (f * g)(n) = Σ_{d|n} f(d) g(n/d). The constant function 1(n)=1 for all n is the identity for multiplication of Dirichlet series: If F(s)=Σ f(n)/n^s and G(s)=Σ g(n)/n^s, then F(s)G(s)=Σ (f*g)(n)/n^s (at least where everything converges). The Möbius function satisfies: (μ * 1)(n) = ε(n), where ε(1)=1 and ε(n)=0 for n>1. So μ is the inverse of 1 under convolution. Translating into Dirichlet series: (Σ μ(n)/n^s)(Σ 1/n^s) = 1, so Σ μ(n)/n^s = 1/ζ(s).
So RH is about zeros of ζ(s). Are there other zeta-like functions with similar hypotheses?

Yes. The broad framework is L-functions. Examples: 1) Dirichlet L-functions Given a Dirichlet character χ mod q, define L(s, χ) = Σ_{n≥1} χ(n)/n^s = ∏_{p} (1 - χ(p)p^{-s})^{-1}. They generalize ζ(s) (which is χ=1). They encode primes in arithmetic progressions. There is a Generalized Riemann Hypothesis (GRH) saying the nontrivial zeros of all L(s, χ) lie on Re(s)=1/2. 2) Dedekind zeta functions for number fields They encode prime ideals and splitting of primes in extensions. 3) L-functions of elliptic curves and modular forms Central to modern number theory, with their own analogues of RH (often proven in function field settings, conjectural over number fields). The RH is the prototype of a much larger “spectral” principle: zeros on a critical line correspond to deep regularity.
You said GRH implies results about primes in arithmetic progressions. What sort of results?

A key result is about the error term in the prime number theorem for arithmetic progressions. Let π(x; q, a) count primes p ≤ x with p ≡ a (mod q), where gcd(a,q)=1. Then one expects: π(x; q, a) ~ (1/φ(q)) li(x). GRH gives strong uniform error terms, roughly: π(x; q, a) = (1/φ(q)) li(x) + O(x^{1/2} log(xq)). (There are more precise versions and variants.) This has many consequences: bounds for the least prime in an arithmetic progression, distribution of primes in residue classes, etc.
Back to ζ(s): is there a way to motivate ζ(s) from primes directly, rather than starting with Σ 1/n^s?

Yes: start from the Euler product and work backward. If you want a function whose logarithmic derivative picks out primes, consider: log ζ(s) = - Σ_p log(1 - p^{-s}) = Σ_p Σ_{k≥1} p^{-ks}/k for Re(s) > 1. Differentiate: (ζ'(s)/ζ(s)) = - Σ_p Σ_{k≥1} (log p) p^{-ks}. This series explicitly involves log p and prime powers. That’s already close to ψ(x), which sums log p over prime powers ≤ x. So ζ(s) is essentially the generating object whose logarithmic derivative encodes prime powers with weights log p. That’s exactly why ψ(x) appears in explicit formulas.
Can we make a more intuitive picture of why zeros create oscillations? Like a toy model?

A toy model comes from inverse transforms and residues, but I’ll keep it intuitive. Suppose you have a function F(s) that is a transform of some counting function f(x). In many contexts, you can recover f(x) by an integral over s (an inverse Mellin transform): f(x) ≈ (1/2πi) ∫ F(s) x^s ds integrating along a vertical line in the complex plane. If F(s) has poles, you can shift the contour and pick up contributions from those poles (residue theorem). Each pole at s=ρ contributes a term proportional to x^{ρ}. That’s the origin of expansions like “main term + sum over zeros.” For prime counting, the relevant transform involves -ζ'(s)/ζ(s), whose poles correspond to zeros of ζ(s). Those poles then contribute oscillatory terms x^{ρ}. Hence zeros ↔ oscillations. Even if you don’t use complex integration explicitly, the moral is: singularities/zeros of the transform determine the decomposition of the original function into main growth plus oscillatory corrections.
You mentioned poles. But zeros of ζ(s) become poles of ζ'/ζ, right?

Exactly. If ζ(s) has a simple zero at s=ρ, then near ρ: ζ(s) ≈ (s-ρ)ζ'(ρ), so ζ'(s)/ζ(s) ≈ 1/(s-ρ). So ζ'/ζ has a simple pole at each zero of ζ, and the residue is 1 (for simple zeros). Those poles are what appear in explicit formulas derived from contour integrals. Similarly, ζ has a simple pole at s=1, so ζ'/ζ has a simple pole at s=1 too, contributing the main term x.
Are the zeros known to be simple?

It’s widely believed that all nontrivial zeros are simple, and numerical computations support that for the many zeros checked. But it is not proven that all are simple. Many results can be stated without assuming simplicity, by counting multiplicities. The explicit formula still works with multiplicities; residues become multiplicity-weighted.
How far has RH been checked numerically?

Many trillions of zeros have been verified to lie on the critical line, up to very large heights (on the order of 10^13 or more, depending on the computation and date). The exact record height changes as computations improve. Numerical evidence is overwhelming, but of course it cannot prove the statement for all zeros.
Is there a reason people expect the critical line specifically, not some other line?

Several reasons converge: 1) Functional equation symmetry The completed function ξ(s) satisfies ξ(s)=ξ(1-s), centering symmetry at 1/2. 2) Analogy with function fields For zeta functions of curves over finite fields, the analogue of RH is true (Weil). Zeros lie on the critical line due to deep geometric reasons. 3) Random matrix theory and statistics of zeros The distribution of zeros’ spacings matches eigenvalue statistics of random Hermitian matrices, which naturally live on a “critical line” in the model. 4) “Best possible” error terms If zeros strayed off 1/2, you’d get larger errors in prime distribution than heuristics suggest. RH aligns with a square-root cancellation philosophy. So 1/2 is not arbitrary; it’s forced by symmetry and consistent with multiple deep analogies.
Can you say more about the function field analogy in a way that doesn’t require algebraic geometry?

At a very high level: - Over the integers, ζ(s) encodes primes. - Over a finite field F_q, you can study polynomials and their “prime” elements (irreducible polynomials), and you can build zeta functions that encode how many solutions a curve has over extensions F_{q^n}. In that setting, the zeta function becomes a rational function in a variable u = q^{-s}. Its zeros and poles correspond to eigenvalues of a finite-dimensional linear operator (Frobenius acting on cohomology). Those eigenvalues have absolute value q^{1/2}, which translates into zeros lying on Re(s)=1/2. So in function fields, there is a clear spectral reason zeros lie on the critical line. RH over the integers is expected to have a similar spectral/operator interpretation, though the precise operator remains conjectural (Hilbert–Pólya idea).
What is the Hilbert–Pólya idea?

It’s a conjectural strategy: find a self-adjoint (Hermitian) operator whose eigenvalues correspond to the imaginary parts of the nontrivial zeros of ζ(s). If such an operator existed, then its eigenvalues would be real, which would imply zeros have real part 1/2 (because they’d be of the form 1/2 + i·(real number)). It’s not a complete plan by itself—you still need to construct the operator and show the correspondence—but it motivates why RH might be “spectral” in nature.
I keep seeing the phrase “critical line” and “critical strip.” Why “critical”?

Historically and conceptually: - The strip 0 < Re(s) < 1 is where the complex-analytic behavior relevant to primes is “in play”: outside it, ζ(s) has no nontrivial zeros (and behavior is more controlled). - The line Re(s)=1/2 is the symmetry axis from the functional equation and the expected location of zeros. “Critical” reflects that this region is the boundary between convergence domains and that the most delicate behavior happens there. It’s also “critical” because the quality of prime distribution estimates hinges on how close zeros get to Re(s)=1.
Can you explain how ζ(s) is defined for complex s in the first place? Like, what does n^{-s} mean?

For complex s = σ + it, define: n^{-s} = e^{-s log n} = e^{-(σ+it) log n} = n^{-σ} · e^{-it log n}. So each term in ζ(s) is: 1/n^s = n^{-σ} (cos(t log n) - i sin(t log n)). For σ > 1, Σ n^{-σ} converges, and the oscillatory factors don’t hurt convergence. In fact, absolute convergence holds because Σ |1/n^s| = Σ n^{-σ} converges. That’s why the series definition works for Re(s) > 1.
That helps. So on the critical line σ=1/2, the series doesn’t converge absolutely. Does it converge conditionally?

On σ = 1/2, the series Σ n^{-1/2 - it} does not converge in the usual sense (it’s not absolutely convergent, and it also fails to converge conditionally in a straightforward way for many t). One needs alternative representations (like the analytic continuation, or the Dirichlet eta function) to define ζ(s) there. A common trick: use the alternating series η(s) = Σ_{n≥1} (-1)^{n-1} / n^s which converges for Re(s) > 0 (by alternating series/Dirichlet test in a complex form). Then ζ(s) = η(s) / (1 - 2^{1-s}) for Re(s) > 0 and s ≠ 1. This gives a way to define ζ(s) in the half-plane Re(s) > 0 except at s=1, without heavy machinery.
Wait, can we derive that relationship between η(s) and ζ(s)?

Yes, using algebra on series (valid where both converge, initially for Re(s)>1, then extended). Start with ζ(s) = Σ_{n≥1} 1/n^s. Split into odd and even terms: ζ(s) = Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s + Σ_{even n} 1/n^s = Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s + Σ_{m≥1} 1/(2m)^s = Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s + 2^{-s} ζ(s). So Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s = (1 - 2^{-s}) ζ(s). Now η(s) = 1 - 1/2^s + 1/3^s - 1/4^s + ... = Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s - Σ_{even n} 1/n^s = Σ_{odd n} 1/n^s - 2^{-s} ζ(s) = (1 - 2^{-s})ζ(s) - 2^{-s} ζ(s) = (1 - 2^{1-s}) ζ(s). Thus ζ(s) = η(s) / (1 - 2^{1-s}).
Nice. So does η(s) have zeros too? Are they related?

Yes. From ζ(s) = η(s)/(1 - 2^{1-s}), zeros of ζ(s) correspond to zeros of η(s), except where the denominator also vanishes. The denominator 1 - 2^{1-s} = 0 when 2^{1-s} = 1, i.e. (1-s) log 2 = 2πik i for integer k, giving: s = 1 - 2πik / log 2 · i. These are points on the line Re(s)=1 where the formula has singularities, but ζ(s) itself is not singular there (only at s=1). What happens is: η(s) also vanishes at those points, canceling the denominator. So η(s) has “trivial” zeros that match those cancellation points. The nontrivial zeros of ζ(s) in the critical strip correspond to zeros of η(s) as well.
If η(s) converges for Re(s) > 0, why do we need analytic continuation beyond that?

Because the most symmetric/global statements (functional equation, trivial zeros, etc.) relate values across the entire complex plane, including Re(s) ≤ 0. Also, understanding ζ(s) near negative integers and connecting to Bernoulli numbers or special values requires continuation. But for many discussions of RH, having ζ(s) defined in 0 < Re(s) < 1 suffices to talk about nontrivial zeros. The full analytic continuation and functional equation are what give the symmetry and locate trivial zeros precisely.
Are there any “almost RH” results? Like partial progress?

Yes, there are several types of partial results: 1) Zeros on the critical line It’s known that infinitely many zeros lie on the critical line (Hardy, 1914). More is known: a positive proportion of zeros are on the line (Selberg, Levinson, Conrey, etc.). The best proportion proven is over 40% (a bit above 41% in some results), though not 100%. 2) Zero-free regions near Re(s)=1 It’s known there are no zeros too close to Re(s)=1, except possibly a “Siegel zero” phenomenon for Dirichlet L-functions (not ζ itself). For ζ(s), there is a classical zero-free region: ζ(s) ≠ 0 when σ ≥ 1 - c / log(|t|+2) for some constant c>0. 3) Density theorems These bound how many zeros can lie in regions with σ > α. They imply that “most” zeros are not too far to the right, though they might still exist. These results yield progressively better unconditional error terms for prime counting.
You mentioned Siegel zeros for Dirichlet L-functions, but not for ζ(s). Why not?

For ζ(s), the character is trivial and the structure is simpler; there is no exceptional real character that could give a Siegel zero. Siegel zeros are hypothetical zeros very close to 1 for L(s, χ) with real characters χ (especially quadratic characters). Their existence would have strong consequences for class numbers and distribution of primes in progressions, but they’re not expected to exist (and would contradict many heuristics). For ζ(s), the known zero-free region excludes zeros extremely close to 1 in a strong way, and there is no analogous exceptional phenomenon.
Can we talk about the Prime Number Theorem proof idea? How do zeros relate to PNT itself?

The PNT is equivalent to saying ζ(s) has no zeros on the line Re(s)=1. More precisely, the key analytic input is: - ζ(s) has a simple pole at s=1. - ζ(s) has no zeros on Re(s)=1. Hadamard and de la Vallée Poussin proved this zero-free line result, and from it deduced PNT. Why does “no zeros on Re(s)=1” imply PNT? Roughly, because the main term x in ψ(x) comes from the pole at s=1, and if there are no zeros on the line Re(s)=1, then when you move contours in the explicit formula, you don’t pick up terms as large as x (which would happen if zeros were too far right). So the error becomes o(x), which is enough to get ψ(x) ~ x, hence π(x) ~ x/log x. RH is a strengthening: not only no zeros on Re(s)=1, but all zeros are as far left as symmetry allows (Re(s)=1/2).
Is there an intuitive reason the pole at s=1 corresponds to the main term x?

In the inverse-transform picture, a pole at s=1 contributes a term proportional to x^1 = x. That’s the dominant growth. More concretely, because ζ(s) behaves like 1/(s-1) near s=1, its logarithmic derivative behaves like -1/(s-1). In the explicit formula for ψ(x), that pole yields the x term. So “pole at 1” is like “there is a main linear growth component.”
Can we discuss the trivial zeros? Why exactly negative even integers?

From the functional equation: ζ(s) = 2^s π^{s-1} sin(πs/2) Γ(1-s) ζ(1-s). The sine factor sin(πs/2) is zero when πs/2 = kπ, i.e. s = 2k for integers k. That gives zeros at ..., -4, -2, 0, 2, 4, ... But ζ(s) has a pole at s=1 and is finite at positive even integers (in fact ζ(2)=π^2/6, ζ(4)=π^4/90, etc.), so those zeros at positive even integers must be canceled by poles/zeros in other factors when you interpret the equation carefully. At negative even integers s = -2, -4, ..., the cancellations don’t remove the zeros, and ζ(s)=0 there. Those are the trivial zeros. At s=0, ζ(0) = -1/2, so the zero from sin(πs/2) is canceled by a pole of Γ(1-s) or something else in the product; indeed Γ(1) is finite, but there are other factors. The full cancellation details are part of the analytic continuation consistency, but the net outcome is: zeros at negative even integers remain.
You said ζ(2)=π^2/6. How do we know that without complex analysis?

ζ(2)=Σ 1/n^2 can be evaluated by classical real analysis methods. One approach uses Fourier series of a periodic function like f(x)=x^2 on [-π,π] and Parseval’s identity, yielding Σ 1/n^2 = π^2/6. Another uses the product formula for sin x: sin x = x ∏_{n≥1} (1 - x^2/(n^2 π^2)), compare coefficients with Taylor series, and extract Σ 1/n^2. These are “complex-adjacent” historically but can be done in real analysis.
Does knowing special values like ζ(2), ζ(4) help with RH?

Not directly. RH is about zeros in the critical strip, while ζ(2k) are values in Re(s)>1 where ζ is well-behaved. Those special values are beautiful and connect to π and Bernoulli numbers, but they don’t directly constrain zeros. However, the functional equation relates values at s and 1-s, so special values at positive integers correspond to values at negative integers, which ties into trivial zeros and Bernoulli numbers. Still, RH is more about the complex analytic structure than about those special real values.
If RH is true, does it tell us anything about gaps between consecutive primes?

Yes, it implies stronger bounds on prime gaps than are known unconditionally, though not as strong as the best conjectures. Under RH, one can show there is always a prime between x and x + C√x log x for sufficiently large x (with some constant C). More refined statements exist. This is much smaller than x, but still larger than the conjectured size ~ (log x)^2 for maximal gaps and ~ log x for typical gaps. So RH gives “primes are never too far apart,” with a gap bound on the order of √x log x.
How does that compare to what is known unconditionally?

Unconditionally, the best proven bounds for gaps are much weaker in terms of x: for example, there is always a prime between x and x + x^0.525 (Baker–Harman–Pintz) for large x, and there are results on primes in short intervals with exponents less than 1. Those are deep and difficult. RH’s √x log x is substantially stronger than current unconditional results, but still far from conjectures like Cramér’s.
Speaking of conjectures, what’s Cramér’s conjecture and how is it related?

Cramér’s conjecture says the maximal gap between consecutive primes near x is about O((log x)^2). More precisely, it predicts: p_{n+1} - p_n = O((log p_n)^2). This is based on modeling primes as random with probability ~1/log x. It’s not implied by RH; RH is weaker in this direction. There are stronger hypotheses (like certain forms of the “pair correlation conjecture” for zeros, or stronger assumptions about zero distribution) that would yield tighter gap bounds closer to Cramér’s scale. So RH gives some control, but not that fine-grained.
You mentioned pair correlation of zeros. What is that?

Pair correlation concerns the statistical distribution of spacings between imaginary parts of zeros on the critical line. If zeros are 1/2 + iγ_n (ordered by γ_n), then one studies the normalized gaps: (γ_{n+1} - γ_n) * (log(γ_n)/(2π)), which rescales so the average spacing is about 1. Montgomery’s pair correlation conjecture predicts that the distribution of these normalized gaps matches that of eigenvalues of random Hermitian matrices (GUE statistics). This is connected to random matrix theory and has consequences for fine properties of primes. RH is about location (all on the line). Pair correlation is about how they are distributed along the line.
Is there any way to “see” primes in the zeros, like reconstruct primes from zeros?

In principle, yes: explicit formulas relate prime-counting functions to sums over zeros. So if you knew all zeros precisely (and some additional data), you could compute ψ(x) or π(x) accurately, which determines primes. Conversely, knowing the primes determines ζ(s) via Euler product and thus determines zeros. So there is a kind of duality: primes and zeros encode each other. But reconstructing primes from zeros is not an efficient practical method compared to direct primality testing, and it’s delicate because you need many zeros to get accurate results at large x.
Can we switch gears slightly? I want to understand why unique factorization gives the Euler product.

Sure. For Re(s)>1: Start with the geometric series identity: 1/(1 - p^{-s}) = 1 + p^{-s} + p^{-2s} + p^{-3s} + ... Now multiply over all primes: ∏_{p} (1 + p^{-s} + p^{-2s} + ...). When you expand this product, you choose from each prime p a power p^{-ks} (possibly k=0). A term in the expansion corresponds to a product over primes of p^{-k_p s} = (∏ p^{k_p})^{-s} = n^{-s}, where n has prime factorization n = ∏ p^{k_p}. Unique factorization says: each integer n corresponds to exactly one choice of exponents (k_p), so n^{-s} appears exactly once. Therefore the product expansion equals Σ_{n≥1} n^{-s} = ζ(s). Convergence requires Re(s)>1 to justify rearrangements and infinite product manipulations, but conceptually it’s “unique factorization turned into generating functions.”
Is there a similar product for 1/ζ(s)?
Yes: 1/ζ(s) = ∏_{p} (1 - p^{-s}) for Re(s) > 1. Expanding ∏ (1 - p^{-s}) gives terms with squarefree integers and alternating signs, which matches Σ μ(n)/n^s. That’s another way to see why μ appears.
Could you define μ(n) again and show that expansion quickly?

μ(n): - μ(1)=1. - μ(n)=0 if n is divisible by p^2 for some prime p. - Otherwise, if n is a product of k distinct primes, μ(n)=(-1)^k. Now expand: ∏_{p} (1 - p^{-s}) = 1 - Σ_p p^{-s} + Σ_{p<q} (pq)^{-s} - Σ_{p<q<r} (pqr)^{-s} + ... This picks out squarefree products of distinct primes, with sign (-1)^k for k primes. That’s exactly μ(n). Non-squarefree integers never appear because you can’t pick p^{-s} twice from (1 - p^{-s}). So: ∏_{p} (1 - p^{-s}) = Σ_{n≥1} μ(n)/n^s.
Great. Now, I’ve seen another prime-counting function: Λ(n), the von Mangoldt function. How does it relate?

The von Mangoldt function Λ(n) is: Λ(n) = log p if n = p^k for some prime p and integer k≥1, Λ(n) = 0 otherwise. It’s designed so that: ψ(x) = Σ_{n≤x} Λ(n). And it appears naturally in: -ζ'(s)/ζ(s) = Σ_{n≥1} Λ(n)/n^s for Re(s)>1. So Λ is the coefficient sequence of the Dirichlet series for -ζ'/ζ. That makes it central: it directly encodes prime powers, and prime powers are convenient in analytic arguments.
Why include prime powers? Why not just primes?

Including prime powers makes the analytic expressions cleaner because logarithmic derivatives of Euler products naturally produce all powers: log ζ(s) = Σ_{p,k} p^{-ks}/k, and differentiating brings down log p and includes all k. Also, prime powers are relatively sparse, and their total contribution is small enough that ψ(x) and θ(x)=Σ_{p≤x} log p behave similarly asymptotically. But ψ has better transform properties and cleaner explicit formulas.
You said θ(x) and ψ(x) behave similarly. How similar?

θ(x) = Σ_{p≤x} log p counts primes with log weights. ψ(x) includes log p for p^k as well. The difference ψ(x) - θ(x) comes from prime powers with k≥2, i.e. p^2 ≤ x. That sum is: Σ_{p^k ≤ x, k≥2} log p ≤ Σ_{p ≤ √x} log p + Σ_{p ≤ x^{1/3}} log p + ... This is at most on the order of √x (times logs), much smaller than x. So asymptotically they share the same main term: both ~ x. Thus, controlling ψ(x) is essentially controlling primes.
If RH implies ψ(x)=x+O(x^{1/2}log^2 x), does it also imply θ(x)=x+O(x^{1/2}log^2 x)?

Yes, essentially. Since ψ and θ differ by O(√x), the same RH error bound transfers (up to adjusting constants and maybe log factors). One typically states: θ(x) = x + O(x^{1/2} log^2 x) under RH as well.
Can we connect RH to the distribution of primes in short intervals?

Under RH, you get strong results about primes in intervals [x, x + h] when h is somewhat larger than √x times polylog factors. For example, RH implies: ψ(x + h) - ψ(x) = h + O(x^{1/2} log^2 x) uniformly for h not too small relative to x. This suggests that if h is much larger than x^{1/2} log^2 x, then the main term h dominates, so there must be primes in that interval (in weighted sense). But proving the existence of a prime in every interval of length as small as, say, x^{0.1} is far beyond RH; RH mainly gives square-root scale control.
Is the exponent 1/2 in RH “best possible”?

In a sense, yes: square-root cancellation is often the natural limit for error terms when you sum many oscillatory contributions. Also, there are known Ω-results (lower bounds on the size of fluctuations) showing the error term in ψ(x) - x cannot be too small. Under RH, the bound is close to optimal aside from log factors. There’s also a heuristic that the error term behaves like about x^{1/2} times a random fluctuation, consistent with zeros on the critical line and random matrix statistics.
What are Ω-results?

If you have an estimate f(x)=O(g(x)), that gives an upper bound. An Ω-result provides a lower bound in the sense that f(x) is at least some size infinitely often. For example, for certain prime counting error terms, one can show: ψ(x) - x = Ω±(x^{1/2} log log log x) or similar (the exact form depends on the function and assumptions). The point is: the error term sometimes gets as large as roughly x^{1/2} times slowly growing factors. So you can’t hope for an error like O(x^{0.49}) uniformly.
You mentioned random matrix theory. Is that just numerology or is there a theorem?

There are both rigorous results and compelling conjectural connections. - The Montgomery pair correlation conjecture is partially supported by theorems: Montgomery proved a form of pair correlation for zeros under RH in a certain range (with restrictions). It matches GUE predictions in that range. - Odlyzko’s computations show strong agreement with GUE statistics for high zeros. But a full theorem identifying zeros with random matrix eigenvalues is not proven. It’s a powerful heuristic framework that predicts many fine-grained statistical properties.
What’s a “high-level” explanation of why GUE should appear?

The rough story is: - Zeros of ζ(s) behave like eigenvalues of a quantum chaotic system (Hilbert–Pólya + quantum chaos ideas). - Eigenvalues of chaotic quantum systems are modeled statistically by random matrix ensembles; for systems without time-reversal symmetry, the relevant ensemble is GUE. - Therefore, zero statistics should match GUE. This is not a proof, but it fits many observed patterns and connects RH to physics-inspired spectral ideas.
Can we discuss a bit about “explicit formula” derivations? I know you said contour integrals, but can we outline the steps conceptually?

Conceptually, one common route: 1) Start with the Dirichlet series: -ζ'(s)/ζ(s) = Σ_{n≥1} Λ(n)/n^s for Re(s)>1. 2) Use a transform identity to relate partial sums of Λ(n) to an integral of -ζ'/ζ. For example, use Perron’s formula, which expresses: Σ_{n≤x} Λ(n) = (1/2πi) ∫_{c-i∞}^{c+i∞} (-ζ'(s)/ζ(s)) x^s / s ds with c>1. 3) Shift the contour left into the critical strip. As you shift, you cross poles of the integrand: - A pole at s=1 (from ζ’s pole) gives the main term x. - Poles at zeros s=ρ of ζ(s) give terms -x^{ρ}/ρ. - Poles at trivial zeros and at s=0 contribute additional explicit terms. 4) Bound the remaining integral on the shifted contour to get an error term. This is the standard “explicit formula as a residue sum” picture.
Perron’s formula sounds like a Laplace inversion. Can you explain it in simpler terms?

It is indeed an inversion principle. If F(s)=Σ a(n)/n^s converges for Re(s)>c0, then Perron’s formula lets you recover partial sums A(x)=Σ_{n≤x} a(n) by integrating F(s) x^s / s along a vertical line Re(s)=c>c0. Heuristically: - x^s = e^{s log x} acts like a kernel. - Dividing by s and integrating picks out the “step function” cutoff n≤x. It’s analogous to how the inverse Laplace transform recovers a function from its transform. You can think of it as a Mellin-transform inversion adapted to Dirichlet series.
If that’s the mechanism, then RH is about where the poles are when you shift the contour, right?

Exactly. The poles you cross correspond to zeros of ζ(s). Their real parts determine how big their residue contributions are (since x^{σ} is the magnitude factor). If all nontrivial poles are at σ=1/2, then the error is around x^{1/2} times logs. If any pole had σ>1/2, it would dominate and worsen the error. So RH is, in this contour-shifting picture, the statement that all nontrivial poles you encounter lie on the critical line.
Are there any other famous equivalent statements of RH that look very different?

A few well-known ones: 1) Robin’s inequality (involving the sum of divisors) Let σ(n) be sum of positive divisors of n. Robin proved RH is equivalent to: σ(n) < e^{γ} n log log n for all n > 5040, where γ is Euler–Mascheroni constant. 2) Lagarias criterion (elementary-looking) RH is equivalent to: σ(n) ≤ H_n + e^{H_n} log H_n for all n ≥ 1, where H_n is the nth harmonic number. These are striking because they don’t mention ζ(s) explicitly, but they’re still deeply tied to it.
That’s wild. Can you sketch why divisor sums connect to ζ(s)?

The divisor sum function σ(n) is related to ζ(s) via Dirichlet series: Σ_{n≥1} σ(n)/n^s = ζ(s) ζ(s-1) for Re(s)>2. More generally, multiplicative functions often have Dirichlet series that factor into products involving ζ(s). Since RH is about fine properties of ζ(s), it can be encoded into inequalities about these arithmetic functions, but the derivations are nontrivial. Robin’s inequality comes from delicate estimates on maximal order of σ(n) and its relation to ζ(s) near s=1, where the pole lives.
Can we go back to the basic idea: primes are irregular but “not too irregular.” Is RH the best possible quantitative expression of that?

It’s one of the strongest and most central such expressions, because it gives near-optimal global error terms for prime counting. But “best possible” depends on which aspect you measure: - For global counts up to x, RH gives square-root type error. - For local behavior (short intervals, gaps), RH helps but not to the conjectured optimal scales. - For primes in arithmetic progressions, GRH gives strong uniformity. So RH is a major piece of the “regularity” story, but there are finer conjectures beyond it.
If someone proved RH tomorrow, what would immediately follow?

Many conditional results would become unconditional, especially in analytic number theory and computational number theory. Examples: - Stronger error terms in prime counting (ψ, π, θ). - Better bounds for the least prime in an arithmetic progression (under GRH, but RH alone impacts some cases). - Improved bounds for class numbers and distribution results that rely on zero-free regions. - Tighter guarantees for algorithms that assume RH/GRH for runtime bounds (e.g., certain primality/proving-related tasks). - Many “assuming RH” theorems in the literature would become theorems. However, it wouldn’t automatically resolve conjectures like twin primes or Goldbach; those need different kinds of input (though RH can sometimes contribute partial progress).
Does RH imply infinitely many twin primes?

No. RH alone is not strong enough to imply twin primes. Twin primes are about correlations between primes at distance 2, which is more about the behavior of primes in pairs than about global distribution controlled by zeros of ζ(s) alone. There are stronger conjectures about zeros of other L-functions and about distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions that relate to such questions, but twin primes are typically approached with sieve methods and deep additive/combinatorial tools.
I think I have the big picture now. But can we do a more grounded step-by-step mini-derivation that shows “zeros imply error terms” with a simpler function than ζ?

A classic simpler analogue is the geometric series and partial sums: Let F(z) = 1/(1 - z) = Σ_{n≥0} z^n for |z|<1. The “counting function” here is a_n=1. If you want the partial sum S_N = Σ_{n=0}^{N} 1 = N+1, you can represent it using a contour integral: S_N = (1/2πi) ∮ F(z) z^{-N-1} /(1 - z^{-1}) dz or various equivalent forms. When you evaluate such integrals, the pole at z=1 is responsible for the main term N+1. If you modify F to have additional poles/zeros, those singularities would contribute oscillatory terms like r^N cos(Nθ) (from complex poles at re^{iθ}). The magnitude is controlled by r. Poles closer to the unit circle contribute larger terms. This is directly analogous: - N corresponds to log x scale, - r corresponds to x^{σ}, - angles correspond to oscillations. So in general generating function theory, singularities determine asymptotics. ζ(s) is a sophisticated generating function; its zeros/poles control asymptotics of prime-counting functions.
That analogy helps. So if the pole is at the boundary, you get main growth; if zeros/poles are near boundary, you get big oscillations.

Exactly. In ζ(s), the boundary between convergence and divergence is Re(s)=1. The pole at s=1 sits right at that boundary and creates the main term. Zeros near Re(s)=1 would create large error oscillations. RH says zeros are far away from that boundary (as far left as symmetry allows), making oscillations small.
One last thing: can you clarify the difference between “trivial” and “nontrivial” zeros in terms of their origin?

Trivial zeros: - They come from the explicit factors in the functional equation (like the sine factor), and their locations are simple and understood: s = -2, -4, -6, ... Nontrivial zeros: - They arise from the deeper analytic structure of ζ(s) itself, not from those elementary factors. - They lie in the critical strip 0<Re(s)<1. - Their distribution is intricate and encodes prime distribution. So “trivial” means “explained by the known auxiliary factors,” while “nontrivial” are the mysterious ones.
Could you summarize RH in a single sentence that captures both the statement and why it matters?

RH says: every nontrivial zero of the Riemann zeta function ζ(s) lies on the line Re(s)=1/2, and this is equivalent to primes being distributed with essentially the smallest possible global irregularity (square-root scale fluctuations) around their expected density.
I’d like to go deeper into one equivalence: RH ⇔ M(x)=O(x^{1/2+ε}). Can you explain why that’s plausible?

Plausibility comes from Mellin-transform relationships between 1/ζ(s) and μ(n). We have for Re(s)>1: 1/ζ(s) = Σ μ(n)/n^s. The partial sums M(x)=Σ_{n≤x} μ(n) are like a “summatory function” of coefficients of 1/ζ(s). General principles in analytic number theory say: - If a Dirichlet series F(s)=Σ a(n)/n^s extends analytically to some half-plane Re(s)>α with controlled growth, then the partial sums A(x)=Σ_{n≤x} a(n) grow like O(x^{α+ε}) (roughly). - Conversely, if A(x) is small, that suggests F(s) has no singularities to the right of some line. Here, singularities of 1/ζ(s) are exactly zeros of ζ(s). RH says zeros are at Re(s)=1/2, i.e. 1/ζ(s) has singularities on Re(s)=1/2 but none to the right. That corresponds to M(x) growing like about x^{1/2+ε}. A full proof uses complex analysis (Perron’s formula and contour shifting) and careful bounds, but that’s the intuitive reason.
Does RH give a sharper bound like M(x)=O(x^{1/2} log^k x)?

Under RH, one can prove bounds like: M(x) = O(x^{1/2} exp(C log x / log log x)) and other forms; getting a clean O(x^{1/2} log^k x) for some fixed k is more delicate and not generally what’s stated as the standard consequence. The equivalence is usually phrased with x^{1/2+ε} for every ε>0, which is robust. There are conjectures that M(x)=O(x^{1/2}) might be true, but that’s stronger than RH and actually the old Mertens conjecture (|M(x)| ≤ √x) is false.
Right, I recall Mertens conjecture was disproved. How does that fit with RH?

The Mertens conjecture claimed |M(x)| ≤ √x for all x. It was disproved (Odlyzko and te Riele showed it must fail, though without giving an explicit counterexample x). This does not contradict RH because RH only implies M(x)=O(x^{1/2+ε}), not the uniform √x bound with constant 1.
