RH allows M(x) to exceed √x by factors like log^A x or even larger slowly growing factors; it just restricts the exponent of x. So Mertens conjecture was stronger than RH and turned out false.
Are there “computable” criteria for RH, like something you could check for a finite range that would imply RH?

No finite check can imply RH by itself, because RH is a statement about infinitely many zeros. However, there are partial criteria: if you verify RH up to some height T, you can get explicit bounds on prime-counting error terms up to some range of x, with explicit constants. This is useful computationally. There are also criteria involving positivity of certain sequences (Li’s criterion, for example). Li’s criterion says RH is equivalent to all Li coefficients being positive. But you’d still need infinitely many checks to conclude RH.
What is Li’s criterion, briefly?

Li’s criterion defines a sequence of real numbers λ_n derived from the zeros of ξ(s) (or from derivatives of log ξ(s)). RH is equivalent to: λ_n > 0 for all n ≥ 1. It’s appealing because it converts RH into a positivity problem. But computing λ_n accurately for large n is hard, and proving positivity for all n is still out of reach.
Thanks. I want to make sure I understand the core: zeros at σ=1/2 correspond to oscillations of size x^{1/2}. Is that literally because x^{σ} is the magnitude?

Yes. In the term x^{ρ}/ρ with ρ=σ+it, we have: |x^{ρ}/ρ| = |x^{σ+it}| / |ρ| = x^{σ} / |ρ|. Because |x^{it}| = 1 (since x^{it}=e^{it log x} lies on the unit circle). So the amplitude is x^{σ}/|ρ|. Thus the largest σ among zeros dictates the maximal potential size of contributions. RH sets σ=1/2, giving square-root amplitude.
And the imaginary part t just makes it oscillate as a function of log x?
Exactly: x^{it} = e^{it log x} = cos(t log x) + i sin(t log x). So as x changes multiplicatively, log x changes additively, and you get sinusoidal oscillations in log x with frequency t.
That’s elegant. Could you recommend a conceptual “next step” topic to study after this overview?

A natural next step is to study: - The Prime Number Theorem proof outline (zero-free line Re(s)=1), including the role of ζ'/ζ and ψ(x). - Dirichlet characters and Dirichlet L-functions, to see how the same machinery explains primes in arithmetic progressions. - Perron’s formula and the explicit formula more carefully, to see exactly how residues produce prime-counting estimates. - Basic complex analysis essentials needed for these: analytic continuation, residues, contour integrals, and the argument principle. If you want to stay as calculus-adjacent as possible, start with η(s) and the functional equation, then learn just enough complex analysis to understand contour shifting and residues.
Could we continue with Dirichlet L-functions a bit? I want a high-level picture similar to what you did for ζ.

Dirichlet L-functions generalize ζ by inserting a periodic multiplicative weight. 1) Dirichlet character χ mod q A Dirichlet character χ is a function on integers such that: - χ(n+q)=χ(n) (periodic), - χ(mn)=χ(m)χ(n) (multiplicative), - χ(n)=0 if gcd(n,q)≠1, and otherwise χ(n) is a root of unity. 2) L-function Define for Re(s)>1: L(s,χ)=Σ_{n≥1} χ(n)/n^s = ∏_{p} (1 - χ(p)p^{-s})^{-1}. 3) Connection to primes in progressions The primes in residue class a mod q are isolated by orthogonality of characters: 1_{n≡a mod q, gcd(a,q)=1} can be written as an average of χ(n)\bar{χ}(a) over characters χ mod q. So information about Σ χ(p) over primes p translates into information about primes in specific residue classes. 4) Zeros matter Just like ζ, the zeros of L(s,χ) influence error terms in prime counting in progressions. GRH says all nontrivial zeros of all L(s,χ) lie on Re(s)=1/2, yielding square-root error terms uniformly in q.
Can you show the orthogonality relation you mentioned?

For characters modulo q, one has (for gcd(a,q)=gcd(n,q)=1): (1/φ(q)) Σ_{χ mod q} χ(n) \overline{χ}(a) = 1 if n ≡ a (mod q), and 0 otherwise. This is analogous to Fourier orthogonality: characters are like exponential modes on the multiplicative group (Z/qZ)^×. Using this, you can express counts of primes in a residue class as averages of character sums over primes.
So GRH would imply primes are evenly distributed among residue classes mod q, with error O(√x log(xq))?

That’s the essence, yes. More precisely, for the weighted function ψ(x; q, a)=Σ_{n≤x, n≡a mod q} Λ(n), one expects: ψ(x; q, a) = x/φ(q) + error. Under GRH, the error is about O(√x log^2(xq)) in common formulations (log powers vary with the exact statement). For π(x; q, a), you get similar bounds with slightly different logs. The key is the √x.
Does RH say anything about the distribution of primes in progressions by itself, without GRH?

RH for ζ(s) alone gives information about primes globally but not the full uniform distribution among residue classes for general q. Distribution in progressions depends on zeros of L(s,χ) for nontrivial χ, so you need GRH (or at least zero-free regions for those L-functions) for strong results. Some limited progressions can be handled with other methods, but the clean square-root error term across all q relies on GRH.
Understood. I think this is enough for now. Could you end with a compact “map” of the main objects and how they connect?

Map of connections: - Primes → Euler product: ζ(s)=∏_p (1 - p^{-s})^{-1}. - ζ(s) → prime powers via logarithmic derivative: -ζ'/ζ = Σ Λ(n)/n^s. - Partial sums of Λ(n) give ψ(x)=Σ_{n≤x} Λ(n), a smoothed prime counter. - Perron/explicit formula connects ψ(x) to poles of -ζ'/ζ: pole at s=1 → main term x, poles at zeros ρ of ζ → oscillatory terms x^{ρ}/ρ. - Real parts Re(ρ) control amplitude: x^{Re(ρ)}. - RH asserts Re(ρ)=1/2 for all nontrivial zeros, yielding near-optimal error bounds: ψ(x)=x+O(x^{1/2} log^2 x), π(x)=li(x)+O(x^{1/2} log x). That’s the high-level skeleton: primes ↔ ζ ↔ zeros ↔ error terms.
I still don't buy that switching doors gives you 2/3 probability. Can you convince me?

Take it as a precise game with explicit rules, because the answer depends on them. Standard Monty Hall rules: 1) There are 3 doors. Exactly one hides a car; the others hide goats. 2) You pick one door uniformly at random (or at least without any information). 3) The host knows where the car is. 4) The host always opens one of the two doors you did not pick, and he always opens a goat door. 5) If the host has a choice (i.e., you picked a goat and the other unpicked door is also a goat), he chooses uniformly at random between those goat doors (or at least by some fixed rule independent of where the car is, but uniform is the cleanest). Under those rules, switching wins with probability 2/3. If you want, we can prove it in multiple ways. Which part do you not buy: the conditioning, the host’s behavior, or the intuition that “two doors left means 50–50”?
The “two doors left” thing. Once he opens a goat, there are two doors, one car. Why isn't it 1/2 each?

Because the host’s action is not “randomly opening one of the remaining doors.” It’s constrained by knowledge: he avoids the car. That makes the event “a specific goat door is opened” more likely when your initial pick was wrong than when it was right. Here’s the quickest proof that avoids any heavy formulas: Before anything happens, your initial pick has a 1/3 chance to be the car and a 2/3 chance to be a goat. - If your initial pick is the car (probability 1/3), then switching loses (because you leave the car). - If your initial pick is a goat (probability 2/3), then switching wins (because the host is forced to open the only other goat, leaving the car behind the remaining unopened door). Nothing about opening a goat changes the initial probability that your chosen door was the car, because the host was guaranteed to open a goat no matter what (under the standard rules). So the probability your initial pick is wrong stays 2/3, and switching capitalizes on exactly that. The “two doors left so 1/2” intuition silently assumes the host could have opened the car with some positive probability, but he doesn’t.
But he does open one door. That’s new information. Shouldn’t information update probabilities?

Yes—if the information is not guaranteed in advance. But in the standard setup, “the host opens a goat door” is not surprising; it was promised. The only potentially informative detail is which goat door he opens when he has a choice. To see why “a door gets opened” isn’t informative by itself, imagine a parallel: - You pick a door. - I promise: “I will open a door you didn’t pick that has a goat.” That promise is always satisfiable, regardless of where the car is. So the fact that I fulfill the promise doesn’t distinguish the cases “you picked the car” vs “you picked a goat.” In both cases, I can open a goat. But if we condition on which specific door I opened (say I opened door 3), then yes, that can carry information—depending on my tie-breaking rule when I have a choice.
Okay, so show me with actual conditional probabilities. Like Bayes theorem. I want to see the math.

Sure. Let’s label doors 1, 2, 3. Assume you initially pick door 1. Let C1 mean “car behind door 1,” C2 “car behind door 2,” C3 “car behind door 3.” Each has probability 1/3. Let H3 mean “host opens door 3.” Under standard rules: - If C1 (car behind your pick), host can open door 2 or 3, each goat. With the uniform tie-break rule: P(H3 | C1) = 1/2. - If C2 (car behind door 2), host cannot open door 2 (car), so he must open door 3 (goat). So P(H3 | C2) = 1. - If C3 (car behind door 3), host cannot open door 3 (car), so he must open door 2 (goat). So P(H3 | C3) = 0. Now compute P(C2 | H3), the probability the car is behind door 2 given that the host opened door 3. Bayes: P(C2 | H3) = P(H3 | C2)P(C2) / P(H3). We need P(H3): P(H3) = P(H3|C1)P(C1) + P(H3|C2)P(C2) + P(H3|C3)P(C3) = (1/2)(1/3) + (1)(1/3) + (0)(1/3) = 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/2. So: P(C2 | H3) = (1)(1/3) / (1/2) = 2/3. Also: P(C1 | H3) = P(H3|C1)P(C1)/P(H3) = (1/2)(1/3)/(1/2) = 1/3. So after seeing the host open door 3, your original door still has probability 1/3; the remaining unopened door has 2/3. Switching wins with probability 2/3.
That calculation used a tie-break rule. What if the host doesn’t choose uniformly when he has a choice?

Good catch. The 2/3 switching advantage does not require uniformity to exist, but the exact conditional probabilities given a particular opened door can depend on the tie-break. However, the overall claim “always switch is 2/3” remains true as long as: - The host always opens a goat door, and - The host always opens a door, and - The host never opens your chosen door, and - The host’s behavior does not depend on your initial choice in a way that encodes extra information about the car’s location beyond the constraints. Let’s separate two statements: A) Unconditional advantage of switching (before knowing which door is opened, just knowing the host will open one goat): This stays at 2/3 under the usual “host always opens a goat and always offers the switch,” regardless of how he chooses among goat doors when he has a choice. B) Conditional probabilities given a specific door opened: These can vary if the host’s choice is biased. If you want, we can model a biased host with a parameter and compute the conditional probabilities.
Yes. Suppose when he has a choice he opens door 3 with probability p and door 2 with probability 1-p. How does that change things?

Great. Again you pick door 1. Define p = P(host opens door 3 | you picked car behind door 1). That’s exactly the tie-break probability in the case C1 (since only then he has a choice). Then: P(H3 | C1) = p P(H3 | C2) = 1 (forced to open 3) P(H3 | C3) = 0 (cannot open 3) Compute P(H3): P(H3) = p*(1/3) + 1*(1/3) + 0*(1/3) = (p+1)/3. Then: P(C2 | H3) = P(H3|C2)P(C2)/P(H3) = (1 * 1/3) / ((p+1)/3) = 1/(p+1). And: P(C1 | H3) = P(H3|C1)P(C1)/P(H3) = (p * 1/3) / ((p+1)/3) = p/(p+1). So if the host opens door 3, switching to door 2 wins with probability 1/(p+1). That equals 2/3 only when p=1/2. But note: that’s conditional on him opening door 3. If he opens door 2 instead, the analogous result will favor door 3 with probability 1/( (1-p)+1 ) = 1/(2-p). In other words, the opened door gives you information about the host’s bias. Still, if your strategy is “always switch to the remaining unopened door regardless of which door was opened,” the overall win probability is still 2/3 as long as the host always opens a goat and always offers the switch. Because the only time switching loses is when you initially picked the car, which remains probability 1/3.
Wait. If the conditional probability depends on p, how can the overall probability still be 2/3? That seems inconsistent.

It’s consistent because the conditional event (“host opened door 3”) itself has a probability that depends on p. When you average over which door he opens, the dependence cancels. Let’s compute the overall win probability of “always switch” explicitly. Switching loses only if C1 (car behind your initial pick). That has probability 1/3. Does the host’s tie-break matter in that case? Not for the fact you lose when you switch: if you picked the car, switching always moves you to a goat. Switching wins if C2 or C3 (car not behind your pick). That total probability is 2/3. And in those cases, switching always moves you to the car because the host must open the only goat among the two unchosen doors, leaving the car as the other unopened door. So P(win by switching) = P(C2 or C3) = 2/3. The conditional probabilities like P(C2 | H3) vary with p, but the unconditional probability of winning by switching doesn’t.
But doesn’t the host’s action change the sample space? After he opens a door, aren’t there only two possibilities left?

There are two physical doors left, but not two equally likely worlds. Probability isn’t assigned by counting physical remaining doors; it’s assigned by how likely each world is given the process that generated your observation. A good way to phrase it: the host’s action is part of the random experiment. The “sample space” includes: - where the car is, - what you picked, - what the host opens (which can be random if he has a choice). When you condition on the host’s action, you’re restricting to those outcomes consistent with the observation, but the remaining outcomes may have different weights. If you want a “counting” argument, we can do it with an expanded list of equally likely outcomes that includes the host’s choice as a random variable.
Do that. Enumerate outcomes so it’s just counting.

Assume: - Car location is uniform among 1,2,3. - You pick door 1 (fixing this doesn’t lose generality). - If you picked the car (C1), the host chooses randomly between opening 2 and opening 3 with equal probability. Then the equally likely elementary outcomes can be represented as: 1) Car at 1, host opens 2 (prob 1/3 * 1/2 = 1/6) 2) Car at 1, host opens 3 (prob 1/3 * 1/2 = 1/6) 3) Car at 2, host opens 3 (prob 1/3 * 1 = 1/3) 4) Car at 3, host opens 2 (prob 1/3 * 1 = 1/3) Now suppose you observe “host opens 3.” That restricts you to outcomes (2) and (3). Their probabilities are 1/6 and 1/3. Normalize: total is 1/2. So conditional probabilities become: - Outcome (2): (1/6)/(1/2) = 1/3 (car at 1) - Outcome (3): (1/3)/(1/2) = 2/3 (car at 2) So switching to door 2 wins with probability 2/3. This is just counting weighted outcomes.
I think my hangup is: if I picked door 1 and Monty opens door 3, why isn't it equally likely that the car is behind 1 or 2? Both seem compatible.

“Compatible” isn’t the same as “equally likely.” You need to ask: how often would we see Monty open door 3 in each scenario? - If the car is behind 2, Monty must open 3. So in that world, you see “Monty opens 3” 100% of the time. - If the car is behind 1, Monty could open 2 or 3. If he chooses randomly, you see “Monty opens 3” only 50% of the time. So the observation “opened 3” is twice as likely to occur when the car is behind 2 than when it’s behind 1. That’s why the posterior odds favor door 2 by 2:1.
Suppose Monty is adversarial. He wants me to lose. Does that change the switching strategy?

It can, depending on what “adversarial” means operationally. If Monty can choose whether to offer the switch at all, or can choose which door to open in a way that depends on where the car is (beyond the constraint “open a goat”), then the observation and even the availability of switching become correlated with the car location. That changes everything. But if the rules still force him to: - always open a goat, - always offer a switch, then he cannot eliminate the 2/3 advantage. The 2/3 comes from your initial 1/3 vs 2/3 split, and his forced reveal doesn’t change that. If you want, we can define a specific “adversarial” policy and compute the optimal response.
Okay. Policy: If I initially choose the car, he opens door 3. If I initially choose a goat, he opens door 2. He still always opens a goat and always offers the switch. What happens?

Let’s fix you pick door 1. Consider car location: - If car at 1 (you picked car), host opens 3 (given policy). - If car at 2 (you picked goat), host cannot open 2 (car), so he must open 3. But the policy says “open 2” when you picked goat; that’s impossible here. So your policy is inconsistent with the constraints in that case. - If car at 3 (you picked goat), host can open 2 (goat), consistent. So that policy cannot be implemented while also guaranteeing “always open a goat” across all cases. Any host policy must respect feasibility: he can’t open a door with the car. Try a feasible adversarial policy: - If you picked the car, host opens door 3 (or 2) deterministically. - If you picked a goat, host is forced (no choice) to open the other goat. In that feasible setup, switching is still 2/3. The only degree of freedom is what he does when you picked the car; that doesn’t affect your win probability under “always switch,” because in that case switching always loses anyway.
Let’s step away from 3 doors. If there are N doors and the host opens N-2 goats, leaving my door and one other unopened, is switching probability (N-1)/N?

Under the analogous standard rules (host knows, always opens goats, always leaves exactly one other unopened door, and if he has choices he uses a rule independent of the car), yes: switching wins with probability (N-1)/N. Reason: your initial pick is correct with probability 1/N. If you switch, you lose in that case. Your initial pick is wrong with probability (N-1)/N. If your initial pick is wrong, the host’s openings eliminate all the wrong doors except the one that has the car (since he can’t open it), so the remaining unopened door must be the car, and switching wins. So switching win probability = P(initial pick wrong) = (N-1)/N.
But if he opens N-2 doors, that’s a ton of information. Surely after opening that many goats it should be almost certain my first pick was wrong, but not exactly (N-1)/N? Why exactly that?

It is “almost certain” in the sense that (N-1)/N is close to 1 when N is large. And it’s exactly (N-1)/N because the host’s reveal is constructed to always be possible regardless of where the car is, and it never reveals the car. All the information is “spent” on telling you which doors are goats among the ones you didn’t pick—something the host can always do without touching the probability that your initial pick was correct. Think of it this way: the host’s openings don’t test your chosen door at all. They only filter the other doors to leave the car door among them. The initial probability your door was correct is still 1/N because nothing about the host’s action is contingent on your door being correct vs incorrect in a way that would change that probability (under the standard always-open-goats rule).
I want a simulation argument, but purely conceptual: like “run it many times” reasoning without actual code.

Consider 3 doors. Imagine 300 rounds. About 100 times, your initial choice is the car (1/3 of 300). - In those ~100 rounds, switching loses every time. About 200 times, your initial choice is a goat (2/3 of 300). - In those ~200 rounds, the host is forced to open the other goat, and switching wins every time. So across ~300 rounds, switching wins ~200 times: about 2/3. Nothing about the host opening a goat changes the partition “initially right” vs “initially wrong.” It only ensures that when you’re initially wrong, the remaining door is the car.

But why is the host “forced” when I picked a goat? There are two other doors; one is car, one is goat. He has to open the goat, sure. But doesn’t that somehow make my initial pick more likely to be the car?

It makes the other unopened door more likely to be the car, not your initial door. When you pick a goat, the host has exactly one door he can open: the other goat. That forces him to leave the car door closed. So his action “points” to the car by elimination. When you pick the car, the host has two goat doors he could open. So his action doesn’t point anywhere; it’s less informative. This asymmetry is the whole story: the host’s constrained action is correlated with the car’s location relative to your initial choice.

Let me try a different angle: conditional probability given “host opens a goat” is trivial because he always does. But conditional on “host opens door 3” we got 2/3 only if he randomizes. If he doesn’t randomize, can switching ever be worse than staying?

Under the rules “host always opens a goat and always offers a switch,” switching is never worse in the unconditional sense: it always wins with probability 2/3 regardless of tie-break. But if you condition on a specific opened door and the host’s tie-break is deterministic and known, then after observing which door he opened you might infer something and possibly do better than blindly switching or staying. Example of extreme deterministic tie-break: if you picked the car, host always opens door 3; if you picked a goat (car elsewhere), his action is forced anyway. In that case, observing “host opens door 2” might imply you definitely didn’t pick the car (depending on the deterministic rule), making switching win with probability 1. Observing “host opens door 3” might make probabilities different. But averaged over all rounds, always switching is still 2/3. If you allow the host’s behavior to depend on the car location in more complex ways, and you only sometimes get offered a switch, then yes, switching could be worse because the offer itself can be a signal. But that’s a different game.
Let's formalize that “offer itself is a signal.” Suppose host only offers a switch when my initial pick is correct. Then switching would always lose. Is that allowed?

That violates the standard rule “host always offers the switch.” If we drop that rule, then yes: the host can make the offer informative and invert the advantage. In your modified game: - If host offers a switch iff your initial pick is correct, then conditional on being offered, P(initially correct)=1, so switching loses with probability 1. So the classic 2/3 result is not a universal truth about “opening a goat door” in isolation; it’s about a specific protocol.
Fine. Back to the standard protocol. I want to see the “odds” argument. People say “odds are 2:1 against your door” and they stay that way. Show that cleanly.

Initially, odds your door is wrong vs right are: P(wrong)/P(right) = (2/3)/(1/3) = 2. So odds are 2:1 against you. Now the host opens a goat door among the other two. Under standard rules, this action is guaranteed and doesn’t depend on whether you were right except for tie-breaking when you were right. But crucially, the host never opens your door and never reveals the car. So the only way for your initial door to become more likely would be if the host’s action were more likely when you were right than when you were wrong. It’s not: if you’re wrong, he has no choice; if you’re right, he has a choice. That actually makes the specific door opened slightly more likely under “you’re wrong,” not under “you’re right,” which pushes probability away from your door, not toward it. In the symmetric random tie-break case, after observing which door he opened, the posterior odds become 2:1 in favor of the other unopened door. That is exactly the Bayes calculation we did.
I still feel like the host opening a door “removes” one losing option, so probability mass should redistribute evenly among remaining options. Why doesn't it?

Because the removed option was not removed uniformly at random. Probability mass redistributes according to how the option was removed. If a door were removed uniformly at random among the two you didn’t pick (and it happened to be a goat), then yes, you’d update differently and might get 1/2. But that’s not the game. Think of a simpler analogy with cards: Deck has 3 cards: A (car), B (goat), C (goat). You pick one card face down. Host, who knows the cards, reveals one of the two you didn’t pick and reveals a goat. Now you can switch to the remaining unrevealed card. Same structure. The fact that the host deliberately reveals a goat is what keeps the 2/3. If instead a blind assistant randomly reveals one of the two cards you didn’t pick, and it happens to be a goat, that’s different: that event is more likely if you initially picked the car than if you picked a goat. So conditioning on it can raise the probability your initial pick is correct. That’s a different protocol.
Let's do that variant. Blind assistant randomly opens one of the two unchosen doors, without knowing. If it's a goat, you can switch. Given we saw a goat, what's P(car behind my original door)?

Good. Again you pick door 1. Assistant randomly opens one of doors 2 or 3 with probability 1/2 each, regardless of contents. We condition on the event that the opened door shows a goat. Compute P(C1 | goat revealed) where “goat revealed” means the randomly opened door is a goat. Prior: P(C1)=1/3, P(C2)=1/3, P(C3)=1/3. Likelihood of revealing a goat: - If C1 (car behind your door), both other doors are goats, so revealing a goat is certain: P(goat | C1)=1. - If C2, then among doors 2 and 3, one is car (door 2) and one is goat (door 3). Randomly opening one gives goat with probability 1/2. So P(goat | C2)=1/2. - If C3, similarly P(goat | C3)=1/2. Total probability of goat revealed: P(goat) = 1*(1/3) + (1/2)*(1/3) + (1/2)*(1/3) = 1/3 + 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/3. Then: P(C1 | goat) = [1*(1/3)] / (2/3) = 1/2. So in the blind-random reveal variant, after seeing a goat, your original door becomes 1/2 likely to be the car, and the other unopened door is also 1/2. Switching doesn’t matter. This directly shows why the host’s knowledge and constraint are essential.
That helps. So the classic problem is basically “host's action is conditional,” which biases the remaining door. But can we express it as “probability mass transfers”?

Yes: Start with probabilities: Door you chose: 1/3 car. The set of doors you didn’t choose: combined 2/3 car. Now the host opens one door from the unchosen set, showing goat, but crucially he does this in a way that guarantees he doesn’t remove the car from that unchosen set. So the entire 2/3 probability mass that “car is in the unchosen set” gets concentrated onto the single remaining unopened door in that set. So your chosen door stays at 1/3, and the other unopened door inherits the full 2/3. That “mass transfer” language is a clean intuition.
Is it legitimate to treat the two unchosen doors as a “set” with probability 2/3? Aren't they separate events?

It’s legitimate because “car is behind one of the two unchosen doors” is an event with probability 2/3. Events can be unions of disjoint outcomes. Initially, door 2 has 1/3, door 3 has 1/3, so the union has 2/3. Then the host’s action effectively tells you which element of that union remains possible for the car: he eliminates one door as definitely goat, but he does not eliminate the car possibility from that unchosen set; he preserves it. So the union probability 2/3 becomes assigned to the remaining member. This is exactly what conditional probability does when an event is ruled out.
But he doesn't “preserve” it; he just opens a goat. You're saying he acts to ensure the car isn't opened, which is “preserving.” That seems like sneaking in the conclusion.

It’s not the conclusion; it’s an explicit rule of the game: the host never opens the car door. If you accept that rule, then the car cannot be behind the door he opened. So the car remains among the unopened doors. Given you didn’t pick the car with probability 2/3, the car is among the two doors you didn’t pick. The host opens a goat door among them, leaving one unopened. Since he never opens the car, the car must be behind the remaining unopened door in those 2/3 cases. So “preserve” means “his procedure is constrained not to reveal the car,” which is exactly the rule.
People sometimes say “switching doubles your chances.” Is that always true?

In the 3-door standard game, switching changes your win probability from 1/3 to 2/3, which is indeed doubling. In the N-door generalization, staying wins with probability 1/N, switching wins with probability (N-1)/N. The ratio is (N-1), so switching multiplies your chance by (N-1), which is much more than doubling for large N. But “doubles” is specific to N=3.
Could there be a strategy that beats 2/3 if I’m allowed to randomize between staying and switching?

Not under the standard rules with 3 doors and a single host reveal. The best you can do is 2/3 by always switching. Reason: Whatever strategy you use after the host opens a door, your final choice is either your original door (stay) or the other unopened door (switch). If you stay with probability q and switch with probability 1-q, your win probability is: P(win) = q*(1/3) + (1-q)*(2/3) = 2/3 - q*(1/3). This is maximized at q=0, i.e., always switch, giving 2/3.
That formula assumes the 1/3 and 2/3 remain after the host opens. But isn't that what we're trying to prove?

Fair. Let’s derive it in a way that doesn’t assume the conclusion. We can compute win probability directly by partitioning on whether your initial choice was correct. Let A = event “initial choice is correct.” P(A)=1/3. - If A happens, staying wins, switching loses. Let Ac = “initial choice is incorrect.” P(Ac)=2/3. - If Ac happens, staying loses, switching wins (because host must leave the car door unopened). Now any strategy that stays with probability q and switches with probability 1-q yields: P(win) = P(A)*P(win|A) + P(Ac)*P(win|Ac) = (1/3)*(q) + (2/3)*(1-q) = (1/3)q + (2/3) - (2/3)q = (2/3) - (1/3)q. No assumption about posteriors needed; just the structural fact about outcomes under A and Ac.
Let's verify “if Ac happens, switching wins.” Is it always true? What if host has a choice?

If your initial choice is incorrect (you picked a goat), then among the two unchosen doors, one is car and one is goat. The host must open a goat door and cannot open the car door. Therefore he must open the goat door among the two unchosen doors, leaving the car door closed. After he opens the goat, the only unopened door besides yours is the car. So switching wins. No tie-break exists in this case; the host is forced.
Okay. Another objection: what if the host sometimes deviates and opens a random door, but “in this instance” he opened a goat. Does switching still give 2/3 conditional on seeing a goat?

Not necessarily. That’s the blind-assistant variant we computed: if he opens randomly and you just happen to see a goat, then the posterior changes and switching becomes 1/2. More generally, if with probability r the host follows standard rules and with probability 1-r he opens randomly without knowing, then conditioning on “he opened a goat” yields a mixture posterior. You’d need to know r to compute the optimal response. So: the 2/3 is tied to the protocol, specifically that the host’s behavior is conditional on knowing where the car is and always avoiding it.

Let's do that mixture quickly. Host knows location, but with probability r he behaves like Monty (always opens a goat), with probability 1-r he chooses randomly among the two unchosen doors, even if it reveals car. But we observe he opened a goat. What is P(car behind my original door)?

Let M = event “host uses Monty behavior,” R = event “host uses random behavior.” P(M)=r, P(R)=1-r. We observe G = “opened door shows goat.” We want P(C1 | G). Again you picked door 1. First compute P(G | C1): - If C1, both unchosen doors are goats. Under Monty, he opens a goat (prob 1). Under random, he also opens a goat (prob 1). So P(G|C1)=1. P(G | C2): - If C2, among unchosen doors (2,3), one is car (2) and one is goat (3). Under Monty: he must open the goat (door 3), so G occurs with prob 1. Under random: he opens goat with prob 1/2. So P(G | C2) = r*1 + (1-r)*(1/2) = r + (1-r)/2 = (1+r)/2. Similarly P(G | C3) = (1+r)/2. Now total P(G): P(G)= (1/3)*1 + (1/3)*((1+r)/2) + (1/3)*((1+r)/2) = 1/3 + (1/3)*(1+r) = 1/3 + (1+r)/3 = (2+r)/3. Then Bayes: P(C1 | G) = P(G|C1)P(C1)/P(G) = (1 * 1/3) / ((2+r)/3) = 1/(2+r). So: - If r=1 (pure Monty), P(C1|G)=1/3. - If r=0 (pure random), P(C1|G)=1/2. Intermediate r interpolates.
Nice. So the classic is a special case. Now, can we settle my original intuition with a simple “tree diagram” explanation?

Yes, a tree that branches on where the car is. Assume you pick door 1. Branch 1: Car behind door 1 (prob 1/3). - Host opens door 2 or 3 (goat). - If you switch, you lose. Branch 2: Car behind door 2 (prob 1/3). - Host must open door 3 (goat). - If you switch, you win. Branch 3: Car behind door 3 (prob 1/3). - Host must open door 2 (goat). - If you switch, you win. So in 2 of the 3 equally likely car placements, switching wins. That’s 2/3. The “two doors left” moment happens after the branching already assigned weights. You can’t reset to 50–50 just because two physical options remain.
But in branch 1, host had two choices, making two sub-branches. Doesn't that mean there are 4 leaves total and maybe 2 wins and 2 losses?

There are 4 leaves, but not equally likely leaves. Leaves and probabilities: - Car1, host opens2: probability 1/6. - Car1, host opens3: probability 1/6. - Car2, host opens3: probability 1/3. - Car3, host opens2: probability 1/3. Switching wins in the last two leaves (total probability 2/3) and loses in the first two leaves (total probability 1/3). So counting leaves without weights is the mistake; you must include probabilities.
Suppose I don't know Monty’s tie-breaking, but I do know he always opens a goat and always offers switching. Should I still switch?

Yes. Even without knowing his tie-break, always switching still wins with probability 2/3 for the reasons above: you win whenever your initial pick was wrong, which is 2/3. Your uncertainty about tie-break only affects how you interpret which door he opened, not whether switching beats staying overall.
What if I can choose my initial door based on some pattern? Like always pick door 1. Does that matter?

Not if the car placement is uniform and independent of your choice. Symmetry: any fixed door is equally likely to hide the car. So your initial success probability remains 1/3. If the car placement isn’t uniform or you have information, then the problem changes. But under the classic assumptions, your initial choice method doesn’t matter.
Let's try a more adversarial-but-consistent host. He always opens the highest-numbered goat door available. Does that change anything?

It changes the conditional probabilities given which door he opens, but not the unconditional switching advantage. Example: you pick door 1. - If car behind 1, available goat doors are 2 and 3, host opens 3. - If car behind 2, available goat door among unchosen is 3, host opens 3. - If car behind 3, available goat door among unchosen is 2, host opens 2. So if host opens 2, you can deduce car is behind 3 with probability 1 (since only C3 causes H2). Switching would win with certainty in that observed case. If host opens 3, then car could be behind 1 or 2. Compute: P(H3)=P(C1)+P(C2)=2/3. P(C2|H3)= (1/3)/(2/3)=1/2. So after observing H3, switching gives 1/2, staying gives 1/2. Yet overall, always switching still wins 2/3 because whenever car is not behind 1 (prob 2/3), switching wins; but notice in the case car behind 2, switching wins and host opens 3; in the case car behind 3, host opens 2 and switching also wins. Summing gives 2/3. This shows that “given which door opened” can vary a lot, but the unconditional remains.

Wait, in that deterministic-highest rule, after seeing H3, you said P(C2|H3)=1/2. That suggests switching after H3 wins 1/2, not 2/3. But if H3 happens 2/3 of the time, wouldn't that drag the overall below 2/3?

Because when H2 happens (1/3 of the time), switching wins with probability 1. Let’s compute overall win with “always switch”: - If car behind 2 (1/3), host opens 3, switching wins. - If car behind 3 (1/3), host opens 2, switching wins. - If car behind 1 (1/3), host opens 3, switching loses. So total win probability = 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3. If you instead compute by conditioning on host action: P(win) = P(H2)*P(win|H2) + P(H3)*P(win|H3). Here P(H2)=1/3, P(win|H2)=1. P(H3)=2/3, and given H3, switching wins if car behind 2, which is probability 1/2. So: P(win)= (1/3)*1 + (2/3)*(1/2) = 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3. So it matches.
Good. Now, can we generalize: If host always opens a goat and leaves K doors unopened including mine, what’s optimal?

Let N doors total. You pick 1 door. Host opens N-K-? Let's define precisely. If finally there are K unopened doors total including your original, then the host opened N-K doors. Assume: - Host knows where the car is, - Host always opens only goat doors, - Host always leaves your chosen door closed, - Host always leaves exactly K-1 other doors unopened (besides yours), and opens the rest. Then: - Probability your initial door is car: 1/N. - Probability car is among the other N-1 doors: (N-1)/N. After host opens N-K of those other doors, the car must lie among the remaining K-1 unchosen unopened doors (if you were wrong) or your door (if you were right). If you are allowed to switch by choosing uniformly among the other K-1 unopened doors, your win probability becomes: P(win) = P(initially correct)*0 (if you must switch) + P(initially wrong)*[1/(K-1)]. If you must pick exactly one door among the K-1, then: P(win) = (N-1)/N * 1/(K-1). If instead you can choose to stay or switch to one of the others, the optimal depends: - Staying gives 1/N. - Switching to a random one of the other K-1 gives (N-1)/N * 1/(K-1). Switching is better when (N-1)/(K-1) > 1, i.e., N-1 > K-1, i.e., N > K, which is true if host opened at least one door. So switching to a random remaining door among the others is better than staying as long as K < N. In the classic case, N=3, K=2, so switching gives (2/3)*1 = 2/3.
If K=2 always, then switching gives (N-1)/N, matching earlier.
Exactly. When the host leaves only one other door unopened, switching transfers all (N-1)/N probability mass to that single door.
Another conceptual issue: Some people say “your first pick is 1/3, the other unopened door is 2/3,” but isn’t that assigning 2/3 to a single door after the host opens one, which seems like magic?

It feels like magic only because you’re compressing a two-door event into one door via the host’s selective reveal. Before opening: - Door 2 has 1/3, door 3 has 1/3, total 2/3 across them. After the host opens one goat among those two: - The opened door is assigned probability 0 of being car (because you saw goat). - The remaining unchosen unopened door inherits the entire 2/3 because the host’s rule guaranteed he would not open the car. So it’s not magic; it’s the effect of conditioning on an event that was selected in a biased way (host avoids the car).
So if I reframe: The host is effectively telling me “the car is not behind this door,” but he chooses which door to say that about. That selection matters.

Exactly. If he were equally likely to tell you “not behind door 2” or “not behind door 3” regardless of where the car is, then you’d get a different update. But because he picks a door that he knows is a goat, the statement “not behind door X” is more likely to be made when the car is behind the other unchosen door than when it’s behind your chosen door.
Let's do a full Bayes table for the 3-door standard case with all conditionals, just to be thorough.

Fix you pick door 1. Priors: P(C1)=P(C2)=P(C3)=1/3. Host action probabilities (uniform tie-break when needed): - If C1: host opens 2 or 3 with prob 1/2 each. - If C2: host opens 3 with prob 1. - If C3: host opens 2 with prob 1. So: P(H2|C1)=1/2, P(H3|C1)=1/2 P(H2|C2)=0,   P(H3|C2)=1 P(H2|C3)=1,   P(H3|C3)=0 Compute marginals: P(H2)=P(H2|C1)P(C1)+P(H2|C2)P(C2)+P(H2|C3)P(C3) = (1/2)(1/3)+0+(1)(1/3)=1/6+1/3=1/2. Similarly P(H3)=1/2. Posteriors: Given H2: P(C1|H2)= (1/2 * 1/3) / (1/2) = 1/3 P(C3|H2)= (1 * 1/3) / (1/2) = 2/3 P(C2|H2)=0 So if host opens 2, switch to 3 wins with prob 2/3. Given H3: P(C1|H3)=1/3 P(C2|H3)=2/3 P(C3|H3)=0 So if host opens 3, switch to 2 wins with prob 2/3. That’s the complete table.
Great. Now, can you address the argument: “But the host could have opened either goat door, so opening one doesn't give info”?

It gives info because “could have” is not “equally likely under all hypotheses.” When your initial pick is the car, he indeed could open either goat door. When your initial pick is a goat, he cannot open either; he must open the only goat among the two unchosen doors. So the space of “what the host could have done” depends on the hypothesis about where the car is. Observing what he did therefore updates the hypothesis probabilities.
Another thing: If I choose a door, isn't my probability 1/3. But after Monty opens a goat, doesn't my door become 1/2 because one wrong option is eliminated? That seems like basic conditioning.

Basic conditioning says: P(C1 | some event E) = P(E|C1)P(C1)/P(E). Here E is not “one wrong option is eliminated” in an abstract sense; E is “Monty opened a specific door and revealed goat.” And because P(E|C1) differs from P(E|C2), you don’t get equal posteriors. If you condition only on “Monty opened a goat door” without specifying which, that event has probability 1 under all Ci in the standard game. So conditioning on it does nothing: your door remains 1/3, not 1/2. The step “one wrong option eliminated” is ambiguous: eliminated by whom and by what rule? The rule matters.
Let's attempt to argue against switching: If Monty knows I will switch, couldn't he choose to open a door in a way that makes switching worse?

Under the standard constraints, no. When you picked a goat, he has no choice; he must open the other goat. When you picked the car, switching is doomed no matter what he opens. So his choice cannot change the fact that switching wins in all “you picked goat” cases and loses in all “you picked car” cases. If he can violate constraints (e.g., sometimes not offer a switch, or sometimes open a door at random), then he can manipulate.
How about a 4-door variant: I pick 1, host opens one goat among the other three, leaving 3 unopened doors total. If I can switch to one of the two other unopened doors (excluding mine), what’s best?

Let N=4. After host opens one goat, there remain 3 unopened: your door plus two others. Staying: win prob 1/4. If you switch to a particular single other door (say always to the lowest-numbered remaining), you need the host protocol to compute conditional, because now the host had more flexibility; he might choose which goat to open based on the car location, affecting which doors remain. If we assume a symmetric standard extension: - Host knows location and always opens a goat. - Host chooses uniformly at random among eligible goat doors to open. Then after opening one goat, the probability distribution among the two other unopened doors is not necessarily equal, but by symmetry between those two unopened doors (given you picked door 1 and one specific door opened), they often become equal if the host's choice is uniform and doesn’t bias between them beyond constraints. But the clean strategy is: switch to a random one of the two other unopened doors. What’s its win probability? Your initial pick is wrong with probability 3/4. If wrong, the car is among the other 3 doors. Host opens one goat among those other 3, leaving 2 other unopened doors, one of which is the car. If you choose randomly among those 2, you win with probability 1/2. So win probability = (3/4)*(1/2)=3/8=0.375, which is better than staying (0.25) but less than the 3-door “switch to the only remaining door” case. If instead the host opened 2 goats leaving only one other unopened door (K=2), then switching would be 3/4.
So the dramatic advantage depends on leaving exactly one alternative.
Exactly. The host concentrating all the “not your door” probability onto a single remaining door is what creates the big leap.
I think I finally accept the standard Monty Hall. But I want one last rigorous statement: what assumptions are minimally needed for 2/3?

Minimal assumptions for the classic 3-door 2/3 switching result: 1) Car placement is uniform among the 3 doors (or at least independent of your initial choice and symmetric). 2) You choose a door without information (or at least your choice doesn’t correlate with car placement). 3) Host knows where the car is. 4) Host always opens exactly one door that: a) you did not choose, b) has a goat. 5) Host always offers you the option to switch to the remaining unopened door. Given these, switching wins with probability 2/3. No assumption about random tie-break is needed for the unconditional 2/3, only for conditional probabilities given a specific opened door.
Good. Could you restate the core proof in one sentence?
Your first pick is wrong with probability 2/3, and when it’s wrong the host’s forced goat reveal leaves the car as the only other unopened door, so switching wins with probability 2/3.
That’s concise enough. But I’m still curious: can we construct a host strategy that keeps “always switch” at 2/3 but makes “sometimes stay depending on which door he opens” do even better?

Yes, if the host’s tie-break rule is deterministic and known, you can exploit which door he opens as extra information and sometimes do better than 2/3. Example: host opens the highest-numbered goat door available (as we discussed). - If he opens door 2, you know car is behind 3 with probability 1, so switching wins with probability 1 in that case. - If he opens door 3, then car is behind 1 or 2 with equal probability 1/2, so staying or switching are equal in that case. Optimal conditional strategy: - If host opens 2, switch (win for sure). - If host opens 3, either choice gives 1/2. Compute overall win probability: P(H2)=1/3, win=1. P(H3)=2/3, win=1/2. Total = 1/3 + 1/3 = 2/3. So that doesn’t beat 2/3. In fact, under the constraints that host always opens a goat and always offers a switch, and you have no initial information, 2/3 is the maximum achievable. The host’s action can’t create more than 2/3 probability of winning because at the start there’s only 2/3 probability the car is not behind your door, and you can’t do better than winning whenever it’s not behind your door (which is exactly what switching already does). Any strategy that sometimes stays will necessarily lose in some of those 2/3 cases.
That last claim sounds like a theorem: 2/3 is an upper bound. Prove it.

Let A be the event “your initial choice is wrong.” P(A)=2/3. No matter what you do after the host opens a door, you can only end on one of the two unopened doors: either your original door or the other unopened door. If A occurs (you initially chose a goat), then the car is not behind your original door. The only way to win in that case is to end on the other unopened door. So to win on all A outcomes, you must always switch. Thus the maximum possible win probability is at most P(A)=2/3, and it is achieved by always switching. More formally: P(win) ≤ P(A)*1 + P(Ac)*1 = 1, but we can sharpen: If you ever stay with positive probability on any A outcome, you lose on those outcomes. So the best you can do is win with probability 1 on A and 0 on Ac (since on Ac switching loses). That yields P(win)=P(A)=2/3. Achievable by always switching.
Okay. I’m satisfied. If I ask you to explain it to someone still stuck on “two doors left,” what’s the best single analogy?

The blind-assistant vs informed-host contrast is usually the clearest analogy: if a random person opens a door and it happens to be a goat, probabilities become 1/2; if someone who knows where the car is deliberately avoids the car, switching becomes 2/3. The difference shows exactly why “two doors left” doesn’t imply 50–50: the method of elimination matters.
Done.
